p: The bank or processor that signs up a merchant and lets them accept card payments, settling the funds into their account.
x: The shop's bank. It vouches to the whole industry that this corner store selling ‘artisanal candles’ is legit. For a fee, obviously.
g: Letting a shop accept cards at all, and getting the money into its bank.

p: A standardised way for two software systems to talk to each other, so a payment app can plug into a bank or processor without rebuilding it.
x: The agreed rules for how one computer is allowed to nag another. It replaced the old method of two developers screaming over Slack.
g: Plugging payment features into an app or site without building the rails yourself.

p: The real-time check that confirms a card is valid and has funds, reserving the amount before the payment is actually completed.
x: The bank squinting at your balance and going ‘…sure, fine’ before it has legally committed to a single thing.
g: Checking a card is good for the money before you hand over the goods.

p: The leading digits of a card number that identify the issuing bank, card type and country, the prefix a [[BIN Attack|BIN attack]] tries to guess its way through and the asset rented out under [[BIN Sponsorship|BIN sponsorship]]. Officially renamed the IIN (Issuer Identification Number) and expanded from six to eight digits, with Visa and Mastercard requiring eight since 2022.
x: The bit of your card number that grasses you up. It reveals your bank, your country and whether you're posh enough for the metal one, all before you've spoken.
g: Routing a payment, spotting the card type, and flagging risky or foreign cards.

p: A forced reversal of a card payment, triggered when a customer disputes it with their bank rather than the merchant. Too many push up a merchant's [[Chargeback Ratio|chargeback ratio]], some are outright [[Chargeback Fraud|chargeback fraud]], and [[Chargeback Alerts]] try to head them off before they land.
x: A customer skipping the manager and going straight to the bank, which always takes their side and never returns the goods.
g: Giving cardholders a way to claw back money when a purchase goes wrong.

p: The process of exchanging transaction details between banks to work out who owes whom, before money actually moves.
x: Everyone at the table agreeing exactly what the bill is. The actual paying happens later, allegedly.
g: Working out exactly who owes whom before any money actually changes hands.

p: The actual movement of funds between banks to complete a transaction after [[Clearing|clearing]] has worked out the amounts. It runs to a [[Settlement Cycle|settlement cycle]] and a [[Settlement Window|settlement window]], reaches [[Settlement Finality|finality]] once irrevocable, and carries [[Settlement Risk|settlement risk]] until it does.
x: The day the money actually moves. It arrives long after your receipt, your bank and your optimism all insisted it already had.
g: Actually moving the money so everyone ends up with what they are owed.

p: An automated message a system sends to your app the instant something happens, so you don't have to keep asking ‘is it done yet?’
x: A system that texts you the second something happens, instead of making you refresh the page like a Victorian widow watching for a ship.
g: Getting told the instant a payment succeeds or fails, so your app reacts in real time.

p: A cryptocurrency pegged to a stable asset like the dollar, designed to hold a steady value rather than swing wildly.
x: Crypto that swore it would stay worth a dollar. Most keep the promise. The ones that don't make excellent documentaries.
g: Moving dollars across borders and crypto markets without the wild price swings.

p: The card network such as Visa or Mastercard that sets the rules and rails connecting issuers and acquirers.
x: The referee who also owns the stadium, prints the tickets, writes the rulebook and takes a cut of the pies.
g: Setting the rules and rails that let any card work at almost any shop worldwide. It funds itself partly through [[Scheme Fees]].

p: The bank that gives a cardholder their card and account, and approves or declines their transactions.
x: The bank whose name is on your card and whose single text message can detonate your evening from across the country.
g: Giving customers a card and standing behind every payment they make.

p: The fee the acquirer pays the issuer on every card transaction, set by the scheme and usually passed on to merchants.
x: A toll you've paid thousands of times, never noticed, never agreed to, going to a bank you'll never meet.
g: Funding card rewards and issuer costs, which is why your points exist.

exp: Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard
p: The security standard every business handling card data must follow to protect it from breaches and fraud.
x: A 300-page checklist that exists solely so your card number never surfaces in a file called ‘customers_final_FINAL_v3_USE_THIS.xlsx’.
g: Keeping card data safe, and keeping you out of breach headlines and fines.

p: Two distinct ideas share the word. In payments, replacing card details with a meaningless stand-in so the real number is never exposed. In crypto, representing a real-world asset like property or a bond as a tradable token on a ledger.
x: Either a nightclub stamp standing in for your passport, or turning a slice of a building into something you can trade online. Same word, very different rooms.
g: Storing cards safely without the real number, and turning illiquid assets into tradable, fractional tokens.

p: An extra authentication step, like a banking app prompt, that confirms it's really you making an online card payment. The merchant's [[3DS Server|3DS server]] starts the check, and passing it earns the merchant a [[Liability Shift|liability shift]] onto the issuer.
x: The pop-up that demands proof of your identity at the precise emotional peak of buying something you didn't need.
g: Cutting online fraud and shifting the liability for it off the merchant.

p: Strong Customer Authentication: an EU rule requiring two independent proofs of identity for most electronic payments, unless an [[SCA Exemption]] applies.
x: Brussels decided one password was too easy. So now everything you buy comes with a small hostage negotiation involving your phone.
g: Forcing strong proof of identity to block most remote payment fraud.

p: The EU payments directive that opened bank data to licensed third parties and mandated stronger authentication, now being succeeded by [[PSD3]].
x: The law that ordered banks to share their toys with fintechs. They complied with all the warmth of a teenager handing over the PlayStation.
g: Opening banks up so fintechs can build account access and payment apps.

p: A framework letting customers securely share their bank data with, or initiate payments through, approved third-party apps.
x: Your bank finally letting other apps peek at your account, with your consent, which you definitely read in full and understood completely.
g: Letting apps read your accounts or pay from them, with your permission. Its broader successor is [[Open Finance]].

p: The Single Euro Payments Area, which makes euro bank transfers across participating countries as easy as domestic ones, whether the instant flavour of [[SEPA Instant]] or the recurring pulls of [[SEPA Direct Debit Core & B2B]].
x: The reason wiring euros to a stranger in Lisbon is as frictionless as paying your flatmate, and roughly as recoverable.
g: Making euro payments across Europe as cheap and simple as domestic ones.

exp: Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication
p: The global messaging network banks use to send secure instructions for international payments. Its modern tracking layer is [[SWIFT gpi]].
x: Not the money. Just the eye-wateringly expensive group chat banks use to discuss it, named ‘Swift’ by someone who'd clearly never used it.
g: Letting banks worldwide send each other secure payment instructions.

p: A standardised international bank account number that identifies an account across borders for transfers. Its disposable cousin, mapped to a real account, is the [[Virtual IBAN]].
x: Your account number after a gap year abroad. Twice as long, vaguely European, and impossible to read aloud without losing your place.
g: Making sure a cross-border transfer lands in exactly the right account.

p: The US system for batched bank-to-bank transfers like payroll and direct debits, processed in bulk rather than instantly.
x: How America moves money. Vast, dependable, foundational to the economy, and still somehow slower than posting a cheque in 1974.
g: Moving payroll, bills and direct debits cheaply in bulk across the US.

p: An arrangement letting a company pull agreed payments from your account on a schedule, with your prior authorisation.
x: A single ‘yes’ from 2014 that the gym you stopped attending in 2015 is still feasting on to this day.
g: Collecting regular bills automatically without chasing customers each month.

p: A stored card a merchant keeps with consent, so a returning customer can pay without re-entering details. The account-to-account equivalent is [[Bank-on-File|bank-on-file]].
x: The reason a 2am impulse buy takes one tap and zero of the friction that might have saved you from yourself.
g: Letting returning customers and subscriptions pay without re-entering a card.

p: The proportion of a merchant's transactions that get disputed, watched closely by schemes as a fraud and risk signal, and pushed up by every [[Chargeback|chargeback]], including the bogus disputes known as [[Chargeback Fraud|chargeback fraud]].
x: The number that, once it creeps too high, summons Visa for a ‘quick chat’ with the energy of a parent who's seen your search history.
g: Telling a merchant, and the schemes, whether disputes are getting out of hand.

p: Know Your Customer: the checks a business must run to verify a customer's identity before onboarding them.
x: Why a shiny fintech needs a selfie, a passport and a utility bill before it will deign to let you deposit your own money.
g: Stopping criminals opening accounts and keeping a firm on the right side of the law.

p: Anti-Money-Laundering rules requiring firms to detect, prevent and report attempts to disguise illicit funds.
x: An enormous global apparatus dedicated to stopping people pretending their cash came from a suspiciously profitable car wash.
g: Catching dirty money before it moves and keeping regulators off a firm's back.

p: When a lender writes off a debt as unlikely to be repaid, though it may still try to collect.
x: The bank formally moving from ‘he'll pay’ to ‘he's never paying’, while still passing your number to people who disagree.
g: Letting a lender clean its books once a debt is realistically unrecoverable.

p: The legal entity responsible for a sale, handling payment, tax, refunds and compliance, even if another party fulfils it.
x: Whoever's name is on the lawsuit, the tax bill and the angry email. Crucially, not the person who did the actual work.
g: Taking the tax, compliance and refund headaches off the actual seller.

p: The underlying infrastructure that moves money from A to B, including cards, bank transfers and real-time systems.
x: The plumbing your salary travels through. Invisible, taken utterly for granted, and the source of pure panic the day it backs up.
g: The track money travels on, so choosing it shapes cost, speed and reach.

p: The UK system that moves money between bank accounts in seconds, around the clock.
x: The genuinely fast UK rail that single-handedly killed ‘the cheque's in the post’ as a viable lie.
g: Sending money between UK accounts in seconds, day or night.

p: Account-to-account transfers that clear and settle instantly, any time of day, with immediate confirmation. Commonly abbreviated [[RTP]], which also names a specific US network.
x: Money that arrives the instant you send it, and therefore the instant before you realise you've sent it to entirely the wrong person.
g: Settling instantly so the payee can use the money the moment it lands.

p: Matching incoming payments against invoices and records to confirm everything that should have arrived actually did.
x: The month-end ritual of proving the money on the screen and the money in the bank are the same money, then quietly weeping over the 14p that isn't.
g: Proving the money you think you received is the money that actually arrived.

p: When a customer falsely disputes a legitimate purchase to get their money back while keeping the goods, an abuse of the genuine [[Chargeback|chargeback]] process that drives up a merchant's [[Chargeback Ratio|chargeback ratio]].
x: Cutely rebranded as ‘friendly fraud’. Friendly to everyone except the merchant it robs blind and the rest of us who pay for it in fees.
g: Helping merchants recognise and challenge bogus disputes instead of just eating the loss.

p: A Payment Service Provider that lets merchants accept multiple payment methods through a single integration.
x: The middle layer that spares a shop from personally befriending every bank on the planet, in exchange for a small slice of everything forever.
g: Accepting lots of payment methods through one integration instead of many.

p: The software that securely captures payment details at checkout and passes them on for processing.
x: The bouncer of the transaction. Frisks your card data, checks the list, and decides whether you're getting in tonight.
g: Capturing card details securely at checkout and passing them on to be processed.

p: Money held in transit between leaving one account and arriving in another, which the holder can briefly benefit from.
x: The cash that belongs to no one for a magical moment, during which a bank is absolutely earning interest on it, thanks for asking.
g: Earning interest or smoothing cash flow while money sits briefly in transit.

p: An account a bank holds in a foreign currency at another bank, to settle international payments locally.
x: A bank's overseas piggy bank, stashed abroad so it doesn't have to physically courier suitcases of cash across a border every Tuesday.
g: Letting a bank settle foreign payments locally without shipping cash abroad.

p: Foreign exchange, converting one currency into another at a rate that includes a spread the provider keeps.
x: The dark alley where the rate Google promised you and the rate you actually got shake hands and quietly part ways.
g: Turning one currency into another so you can pay or get paid across borders.

p: The difference between the buy and sell price of a currency or asset, which is how the provider makes money.
x: The polite, invisible gap between the price you were quoted and the price you were charged. Yes, someone's holiday is funded by it.
g: How providers make money on currency, so knowing it helps you compare real costs.

p: When an issuer refuses a transaction, for insufficient funds, suspected fraud, or a technical reason.
x: The terminal's flat, public ‘no’. Delivered at full volume, to a growing queue, while you mutter ‘try it again’ with dying confidence.
g: Stopping a payment that lacks funds or looks fraudulent before it completes.

p: A temporary refusal that may succeed if retried, often caused by authentication or connectivity issues.
x: A ‘no’ that's secretly a ‘maybe’. The transaction equivalent of being left on read, then replied to ten minutes later.
g: Recovering a sale by retrying a payment that failed for a fixable reason.

p: A transaction where the physical card is tapped, inserted or swiped in person, carrying lower fraud risk, the in-person counterpart to [[Card Not Present|card not present]].
x: The wholesome, old-fashioned kind where the card is actually there, in a hand, in a shop, like our ancestors intended.
g: Lower fraud risk and cheaper fees when the card is physically there.

p: An online or phone transaction where the card isn't physically used, carrying higher fraud risk and fees, the remote counterpart to [[Card Present|card present]].
x: The shifty cousin of in-person payments, where everyone just has to take a string of numbers at its word and pray.
g: Accepting payments online or by phone when the card cannot be seen.

p: Assessing the risk of taking on a merchant or borrower before approving them for an account or credit.
x: A stranger calmly calculating the precise odds that you'll ruin their year, then charging you extra for the privilege.
g: Deciding who to take on, and pricing the risk before it bites you.

p: A card that must be paid off in full each month, with no option to carry a balance like a credit card.
x: A credit card with crippling commitment issues. It flat-out refuses to let the relationship carry over into next month.
g: Spending flexibly each month while being forced to clear the balance in full.

p: The authoritative record of all transactions and balances that determines who has what.
x: The one document in the building whose opinion matters. Argue with it all you like. It has never once been wrong about who's broke.
g: The single source of truth for who has what, that every system trusts.

p: A design principle ensuring that repeating the same request, like a double-clicked payment, only takes effect once.
x: The unsung hero that ensures panic-jabbing ‘Pay Now’ five times charges you once, not five times and a small breakdown.
g: Making sure a retried or double-clicked payment only charges once.

p: Two senses now. Classically, the standing permission you give a biller to collect [[Direct Debit|direct debits]] from your account on agreed terms. Increasingly, in [[Agentic Commerce|agentic commerce]], a cryptographically signed instruction proving exactly what you authorised an AI agent to buy.
x: One word, two leashes: the long-standing permission letting a company dip into your account, and the shiny new digital one letting your AI agent spend on your behalf.
g: Authorising repeat collections, or, in its newer sense, bounding what an autonomous agent may purchase.

p: The scheduled period in which batched transactions are processed and funds moved between parties, one slot within the broader [[Settlement Cycle|settlement cycle]] and closing at a fixed [[Cut-Off Time|cut-off time]].
x: The cut-off you missed by nine minutes on a Friday, instantly transforming a small task into a full weekend of dread.
g: Knowing exactly when batched payments process and funds actually move.

p: A temporary hold placed on funds to guarantee availability, later captured or released, common with hotels and fuel.
x: The hotel silently freezing a chunk of your holiday budget ‘just in case’ you make off with the dressing gown and a mini Toblerone.
g: Guaranteeing funds are there before the final amount is known, as at hotels.

p: The step that turns an authorisation into an actual charge, telling the bank to move the reserved funds.
x: The moment a vague ‘we're holding some of your money’ hardens into a very specific, non-negotiable ‘that'll be £80’.
g: Turning an approved hold into an actual charge once you ship or deliver.

p: A digital store of payment credentials like Apple Pay or a crypto wallet, used to pay without the physical card.
x: All your cards reincarnated inside your phone. Beautifully convenient, right up until you drop it down the toilet and lose every one at once.
g: Paying online or in store from your phone without carrying physical cards.

p: Financial services built directly into non-financial apps, so you can pay, borrow or insure without leaving them.
x: The quiet moment your taxi app, coffee app or shopping app becomes a bank, takes your deposits, and never once mentions it.
g: Letting any app offer payments or credit without becoming a bank.

p: A layer that routes each payment to the best processor automatically, improving acceptance rates and resilience.
x: An air-traffic controller for your payments, calmly steering each one away from whichever processor is currently, quietly, on fire.
g: Routing each payment to the best processor to lift approvals and dodge outages.

p: Cancelling a card authorisation before it is captured, so the hold is released and no money ever moves.
x: The transaction's undo button, usable only in the brief window before the money becomes real.
g: Cancelling a mistaken or duplicate charge before any money leaves the customer.

p: Returning funds to a customer for a purchase that has already been captured and settled.
x: Money going home. Always slower to arrive than it ever was to leave.
g: Returning money cleanly when a customer cancels or returns a purchase.

p: Unwinding a transaction message shortly after it is sent, usually to fix an error or a duplicate.
x: The system quietly pretending that last thing never happened, and mostly getting away with it.
g: Quickly correcting a wrong or duplicated transaction before it settles.

p: When an issuer approves only part of the requested amount because the rest of the funds are not available.
x: The card meeting you halfway, which is somehow more awkward than a flat no.
g: Letting a sale go through on prepaid or low-balance cards for what they can cover.

p: Adding to an existing hold as the final bill grows, common with hotels, fuel and car hire.
x: The running tab, formalised. Every minibar Toblerone dutifully appended.
g: Holding more funds as a tab grows, for hotels, fuel and car hire.

p: A four-digit code that classifies a merchant's line of business, used for fees, rewards and risk.
x: The number that decides whether your local pub counts as dining, drinking, or a mystery.
g: Deciding fees, rewards eligibility and risk based on what a merchant sells.

p: Two meanings worth separating: the full card number that routes a payment, and, in India, the Permanent Account Number that acts as a tax ID in onboarding and [[KYC]].
x: Sixteen digits you guard with your life on a card, or, in India, the tax ID a fintech wants before it will so much as say hello.
g: Identifying and routing a card, and, in India, verifying a customer's tax identity.

p: The short code on a card used to prove the cardholder physically has it during an online payment. Visa calls it the CVV (Card Verification Value); Mastercard calls the very same thing the CVC (Card Verification Code), while American Express uses a four-digit CID printed on the front.
x: Three little digits standing between a thief and your savings. No pressure. (Four, and on the front, if you bank with American Express.)
g: Proving a shopper actually holds the card during an online purchase.

p: A fraud check comparing the numbers in a cardholder's billing address against the issuer's records.
x: The reason a single typo in your postcode can brand you a criminal at checkout.
g: Cheaply screening out fraud by checking the billing address matches.

p: A long unique reference assigned to a settled card transaction, used to trace it across the network.
x: The tracking number for your money, quoted at you by support staff who also cannot find it.
g: Tracing exactly where a payment or refund went when something goes missing.

p: The short code an issuer returns to confirm that a transaction has been approved.
x: The little yes the bank mutters, kept by everyone in case it later claims it never said it.
g: Proving a transaction was approved if it is ever queried later.

p: A group of captured transactions submitted together, usually at end of day, for clearing and settlement.
x: A whole day's takings bundled up and sent off at closing, like a packed lunch.
g: Submitting a day's sales together so they clear and settle efficiently.

p: A flow where authorisation and clearing are sent as two separate messages, common for credit cards.
x: Asking permission first and taking the money later, like a remarkably polite burglar.
g: Separating approval from capture so merchants can ship before charging.

p: The share of attempted transactions that issuers approve rather than decline.
x: The number every merchant obsesses over and every customer feels personally, in a queue.
g: The headline number for how much revenue you win or lose at checkout.

p: A provider that holds one master merchant account and rapidly onboards many smaller businesses underneath it as [[Sub-merchant|sub-merchants]], a lighter-touch sibling of the [[Payment Aggregator|payment aggregator]].
x: The landlord of payments, subletting its merchant account to tenants who skip the paperwork.
g: Onboarding small merchants in minutes instead of weeks of bank paperwork.

p: A third party that resells a bank's card-acquiring services without holding the merchant's funds.
x: The middleman's middleman, earning a slice for the introduction and little else.
g: Reselling acquiring to merchants a bank would not chase itself.

p: A business that takes card payments under a [[Payment Facilitator|payment facilitator]]'s master account rather than its own.
x: A merchant with training wheels, trading under someone else's name and risk appetite.
g: Letting a small seller take cards fast under someone else's account.

p: The technology provider that routes authorisation and clearing messages between merchants, networks and banks.
x: The courier of the payments world. Invisible when it works, cursed when it does not.
g: The plumbing that actually moves authorisation and clearing messages around.

p: The unique identifier an acquirer assigns to a merchant to track their transactions.
x: Your shop's payments passport number. Lose it and nobody knows who got paid.
g: Identifying which merchant a payment belongs to for routing and payout.

p: The standard card structure of cardholder, [[Issuer|issuer]], [[Acquirer|acquirer]] and merchant, with the network in the middle, as opposed to the closed-loop [[Three-Party Model|three-party model]].
x: The polite fiction that paying for a coffee involves only four parties and not a small committee.
g: The structure that lets any bank's card work at any merchant's till.

p: A closed-loop card model where one company acts as both issuer and acquirer, such as American Express, as opposed to the open [[Four-Party Model|four-party model]] where those roles sit at different banks.
x: A card scheme that cut out the middlemen by simply being all of them at once.
g: Tighter control and richer data by owning both sides of the card.

p: The global chip-card standard that authenticates cards cryptographically to cut counterfeit fraud.
x: The chip that ended the golden age of the back-alley card cloner.
g: Killing counterfeit card fraud at the physical [[Point of Sale|point of sale]].

p: Card-present authentication using the [[EMV]] chip together with a cardholder PIN.
x: The ritual that replaced the signature nobody ever actually checked.
g: Verifying the cardholder in person far more securely than a signature.

p: Paying by tapping a card or device near a reader, with no insert, swipe or PIN for small amounts.
x: The tap that made spending forty pounds feel like it cost nothing at all.
g: Paying in under a second for everyday purchases without a PIN.

p: The short-range wireless technology that lets a card or phone talk to a reader for contactless payments.
x: How your phone and the card machine have a brief, private conversation about your money.
g: The wireless link that makes tap-to-pay and phone wallets work.

p: The legacy black stripe on a card that stores data and is read by swiping, now being retired.
x: The brown stripe of the 1970s, still clinging to the back of your card out of pure nostalgia.
g: Backwards compatibility for old terminals while the world moves to chips.

p: The physical device at the [[Point of Sale|point of sale]] that reads cards and starts the transaction.
x: The little machine you tap, turn away from politely, and silently beg to say approved.
g: Taking card payments in person, the device at the heart of any till.

p: A small card reader paired with a phone or tablet so a business can take payments anywhere.
x: How the market stall and the plumber finally started taking cards.
g: Letting tiny businesses and tradespeople take cards anywhere with a phone.

p: Software that turns an ordinary contactless phone into a card terminal with no extra hardware.
x: The card machine, dematerialised. Your phone moonlighting as a till.
g: Accepting contactless on a phone with no extra hardware to buy.

p: Branded [[SoftPOS]] that lets a merchant accept contactless payments directly on their own phone.
x: Just tap it on my phone, graduating from dodgy to official.
g: Turning a merchant's own phone into a card machine instantly.

p: The point where a sale is completed, or the system that manages the checkout.
x: Where the shopping ends and the paying begins, usually next to the impulse chocolate.
g: Where the sale and the payment actually happen, in store or online.

p: Payment input boxes served from the provider's secure systems, so card data never touches the merchant's site.
x: Letting a specialist hold the dangerous bit so you never have to babysit a card number.
g: Taking cards online while keeping card data, and [[PCI Scope|PCI scope]], off your servers.

p: Scheme-issued tokens that replace the real card number and update automatically when the card changes, the scheme's own flavour of [[Tokenisation|tokenisation]], each carrying a [[PAR]] that links every token back to a single underlying card.
x: A card number that quietly reissues itself, sparing everyone the your-card-expired email.
g: Keeping stored cards working when they expire, lifting approval rates.

p: The card networks' branded online checkout that removes manual card entry across participating sites.
x: The industry's umpteenth attempt to make you never type a card number again.
g: Letting shoppers check out across sites without retyping card details.

p: A [[3-D Secure]] path where the issuer approves authentication on risk data alone, with no challenge, the opposite of the [[Challenge Flow|challenge flow]].
x: The good ending of online checkout, where the bank decides you are obviously you and waves you through.
g: Approving a payment with no extra steps when the risk looks low.

p: A [[3-D Secure]] path where the cardholder must actively verify, often via an app or one-time code, as opposed to the no-questions-asked [[Frictionless Flow|frictionless flow]].
x: The bit where your bank app demands a hostage check at the worst possible moment.
g: Adding a verification step only when a payment looks risky enough to need it.

p: A scheme service that refreshes stored card details automatically when cards are reissued or expire.
x: The quiet service that keeps your subscriptions alive long after your old card died.
g: Keeping subscriptions alive when a customer's card is reissued.

p: The automated process of retrying and chasing failed recurring payments to recover the revenue.
x: The polite, relentless emails a subscription sends when your card bounces. Email one of many.
g: Recovering revenue automatically when recurring card payments fail.

p: Automatically charging a customer on a repeating schedule, as with subscriptions, usually as a [[MIT|merchant-initiated]] charge on a [[Card-on-File|stored card]]. The account-to-account equivalents are [[Direct Debit]], the [[Standing Order]] and [[Open Banking|open banking]]'s [[VRP|variable recurring payments]], whether [[Commercial VRP|commercial]] or [[Sweeping VRP|sweeping]]. When a charge fails it triggers [[Dunning|dunning]], and the [[DRP|debit recovery process]] chases what is still owed.
x: The reason you are still paying for four streaming services and watching one.
g: Charging subscribers on schedule without manual invoicing.

p: When a shopper starts checkout but leaves before completing the purchase.
x: The digital trolley left in the middle of the aisle, full of things you nearly wanted.
g: Measuring lost sales at checkout so you can win them back.

p: A shareable link that opens a ready-made checkout, so a business can get paid without building a website.
x: Getting paid by sending a URL, which is either wonderfully simple or mildly suspicious.
g: Getting paid by sharing a link, with no website or integration needed.

p: A merchant's response submitting evidence to contest a [[Chargeback|chargeback]] and try to recover the funds.
x: The merchant's appeal to a court that has already mostly made up its mind.
g: Recovering money by contesting a chargeback you believe is wrong.

p: A cardholder's formal challenge to a charge, which can escalate into a [[Chargeback|chargeback]].
x: The opening shot of I did not buy this. Sometimes even true.
g: Giving customers a route to challenge a charge they do not recognise.

p: Fraud committed by the genuine account holder using their own identity, including [[Friendly Fraud|friendly fraud]].
x: The call coming from inside the house. The customer is the culprit.
g: Helping merchants tell genuine disputes from customers quietly gaming the system.

p: When a fraudster seizes control of a real user's account to make unauthorised payments.
x: A stranger moving into your account and spending like they own the place.
g: Helping firms spot hijacked accounts and lock them down before money moves.

p: Fraudsters running lots of tiny transactions to find which stolen card numbers still work, often alongside a [[BIN Attack|BIN attack]] that guesses valid numbers in the first place.
x: Criminals quality-checking their stolen goods, one suspicious one-penny charge at a time.
g: Helping fraud teams catch stolen-card probing early, before the losses scale.

p: Fraud rules that flag too many transactions from one card, device or IP in a short period.
x: The bouncer who notices the same card trying the door eleven times in a minute.
g: Blocking fraud by flagging suspiciously rapid bursts of transactions.

p: When successful [[3-D Secure]] authentication moves fraud [[Chargeback|chargeback]] liability from the merchant to the issuer.
x: The great hot-potato moment where not my problem becomes contractually true.
g: Moving fraud losses to the issuer when proper authentication is used.

p: Encrypting card data from the moment of capture until it reaches the processor, shrinking [[PCI Scope|PCI scope]].
x: Wrapping the card number in an armoured van the instant it is read, so nobody else can peek.
g: Cutting PCI scope and breach risk by encrypting card data at the reader.

p: Fees the [[Scheme|card networks]] charge for using their rails, separate from [[Interchange|interchange]], which goes to the issuer, and from the [[Assessment Fee|assessment fee]] levied per transaction.
x: The network's cover charge, levied simply for the privilege of being on the guest list.
g: Knowing the network's cut so you can read your true cost of acceptance.

p: The total percentage a merchant pays to accept a card payment, bundling all the underlying costs, chiefly [[Interchange|interchange]] and [[Scheme Fees|scheme fees]], as set out in its [[MDR Composition]].
x: The all-in number a merchant actually feels, after the industry has taken its many small bites.
g: The single all-in rate that tells a merchant what cards really cost them.

p: Transparent pricing that passes through the [[Interchange|interchange]] cost at wholesale and adds a separate, visible processor markup, the 'plus'. [[Interchange++]] goes a step further by also splitting out [[Scheme Fees|scheme fees]] as a third line.
x: The honest menu, where you finally see the wholesale rate and the processor's cut laid out separately.
g: Seeing exactly what you pay so you can negotiate and benchmark fees.

p: A single bundled rate per transaction that hides the underlying [[Interchange|interchange]] and scheme costs, the opaque cousin of transparent [[Interchange-Plus|interchange-plus]] and the bucketed [[Tiered Pricing|tiered pricing]].
x: One tidy number with all the awkward details swept comfortably underneath it.
g: Simple, predictable pricing for merchants who want one flat number.

p: Adding a fee at checkout to pass card-acceptance costs on to the paying customer, where allowed.
x: The we accept cards, reluctantly, for a small fee school of customer service.
g: Passing card costs to customers instead of absorbing them, where legal.

p: Offering a card user abroad the choice to be charged in their home currency, usually at a worse rate.
x: The airport exchange rate, smuggled into checkout. Always press pay in local currency.
g: Knowing to refuse it, because paying in local currency is cheaper.

p: Sending a transaction over whichever eligible network costs the merchant the least to process.
x: The merchant quietly shopping around for the cheapest road every time you tap.
g: Cutting a merchant's fees by sending each tap down the cheapest network.

p: A payment sent directly from one bank account to another, bypassing the card networks entirely.
x: Cutting out the cards and just shoving money straight from one account to the next.
g: Paying straight from a bank account, often cheaper and faster than cards.

p: A transfer the payer actively sends, as opposed to one a biller [[Pull Payment|pulls]] from their account.
x: You throwing the money. Far harder to get back than to send, as scammers well know.
g: Sending money you control, with no biller able to pull more than agreed.

p: A transfer where the payee, with prior permission, draws funds from the payer's account, the opposite of a [[Push Payment|push payment]].
x: Someone else reaching into your account, with a permission slip you definitely signed.
g: Letting trusted billers collect what you owe without you lifting a finger.

p: A payer-initiated instruction that pushes funds from their account to the payee's.
x: The humble bank transfer, doing quietly what cards do with great fanfare.
g: Moving money between accounts simply, the backbone of bank payments.

p: A customer instruction to pay a fixed amount to the same payee on a regular schedule.
x: A direct debit you actually control, which is exactly why companies prefer you never use one.
g: Automating fixed regular payments you stay fully in control of.

p: A high-value, usually same-day electronic transfer between banks for large or urgent payments.
x: How you move serious money, with serious fees and a form that takes itself very seriously.
g: Sending large sums same-day when speed and certainty matter most.

p: The instant version of [[SEPA]], formally the SEPA Instant [[Credit Transfer]] (SCT Inst), settling euro transfers in under ten seconds, around the clock, across the area. Its pull-based sibling is [[SEPA Direct Debit Core & B2B|SEPA Direct Debit]].
x: SEPA, but it finally stopped clocking off at five and taking the weekend.
g: Settling euro transfers in seconds, any time, across the continent.

p: The UK system for direct debits and credits that clears over a three-working-day cycle.
x: The unhurried British rail that still thinks three days is a perfectly reasonable wait.
g: Running UK payroll and direct debits cheaply at huge scale.

p: The UK's same-day system for high-value sterling payments, used for time-critical transfers.
x: How you send the deposit on a house, sweating, on completion day.
g: Moving big, time-critical sterling payments same day, like house purchases.

p: A system that settles payments one by one and instantly in central bank money, with no netting, the real-time counterpart to [[Deferred Net Settlement|deferred net settlement]] and a route to [[Settlement Finality|settlement finality]] the moment each payment clears.
x: The grown-ups' rail, where each big payment settles on its own and final, no IOUs.
g: Settling high-value payments instantly and finally in central bank money.

p: A faster [[ACH]] option that clears and settles eligible US transfers within the same business day.
x: America discovering that money could, in fact, move before Thursday.
g: Speeding up US bank transfers without paying for a wire.

p: The body that governs the rules and standards of the US [[ACH]] network.
x: The rule-keeper of America's batch payments, herding the banks into rough agreement.
g: Keeping the US ACH network consistent, safe and interoperable.

p: The US Federal Reserve's instant [[Payment Rail|payment rail]], launched in 2023, settling 24/7 in central bank money.
x: America's central bank finally building the always-on rail the rest of the world already had.
g: Giving US banks an always-on rail to settle instantly in central bank money.

p: Two senses: the general term for [[Real-Time Payments|instant, around-the-clock account-to-account payments]], and specifically the US network of that name run by The [[Clearing House]] since 2017.
x: One acronym doing two jobs: a whole category of instant payments, and the bank-built US network that beat the Fed to it by six years.
g: Talking about instant payments in general, and naming the specific US rail businesses use to move money 24/7.

p: Brazil's central-bank instant payment system, using aliases and QR codes, now used almost universally there.
x: Brazil's runaway success that made cash and cards look quaint almost overnight.
g: Paying instantly by QR or alias, which is why Brazil largely ditched cash.

p: India's real-time system enabling instant bank-to-bank payments through simple virtual addresses, building on earlier rails like [[IMPS]] and [[NEFT]] and working alongside the homegrown card network [[RuPay]].
x: The rail that turned a billion people into instant payers and the rest of the world envious.
g: Letting a billion people pay instantly from a phone with a simple ID.

p: Australia's real-time, pre-authorised account debit service on the [[NPP]], built as a modern alternative to [[Direct Debit|direct debit]], the recurring-payments counterpart to [[PayID]].
x: The direct debit, reborn with consent you can actually see and cancel.
g: Modern, cancellable account debits that beat clunky old direct debit.

p: A check confirming a payee's name matches their account before a transfer, to cut fraud and errors, the EU's broader take on the UK's [[Confirmation of Payee]].
x: The system finally asking are you sure that is the right person, before you wire your rent to a stranger.
g: Stopping misdirected and scam payments by checking the name first.

p: A BIS-led plan to interlink countries' instant payment systems for cheaper, faster [[Cross-Border Payments|cross-border payments]].
x: The dream of plugging every country's instant rail into every other. Adapters not included.
g: Making cross-border payments cheap and instant by linking national rails.

p: Extending [[Open Banking|open banking]]'s data-sharing beyond payments to savings, pensions, investments and insurance.
x: Open banking eyeing up the rest of your financial life and rubbing its hands.
g: Letting apps use your wider financial data for better advice and products. Its cross-economy cousin, covering energy, telecoms and more, is [[Smart Data]].

p: The proposed third EU payments directive updating licensing and fraud rules, expected later this decade, the successor to [[PSD2]] (and accompanied by the [[PSR]]).
x: The sequel to the sequel, arriving with all the urgency EU lawmaking is famous for.
g: Tightening fraud rules and licensing as [[Open Banking|open banking]] matures.

p: A licensed provider that accesses bank-account data or initiates payments on a customer's behalf, the umbrella term covering the account-reading [[AISP]], the payment-initiating [[PISP]] and the funds-checking [[CBPII]].
x: The authorised stranger your bank now has to let in the moment you say so.
g: The licensed firms allowed to access accounts or initiate payments for you.

p: A regulated provider that initiates account-to-account payments straight from your bank with your consent, by offering a [[PIS|Payment Initiation Service]]. It is one type of [[Third-Party Provider|third-party provider]], alongside the read-only [[AISP]].
x: The app that pays from your bank without ever touching a card, or your patience.
g: Paying directly from your bank inside an app, with no card required.

p: A regulated provider that, with consent, reads your account information from your bank by offering an [[AIS|Account Information Service]], one type of [[Third-Party Provider|third-party provider]] alongside the payment-initiating [[PISP]].
x: The app allowed to peer into your accounts so it can tell you what you already feared.
g: Pulling your accounts into one place for budgeting, lending or accounting.

p: A hardened, OAuth-based security standard for protecting high-risk financial [[API]] access.
x: The bouncer's bouncer, guarding the doors that open banking left ajar.
g: Keeping open-banking access secure enough for real money to flow.

p: An open-banking setup letting a trusted party make recurring payments from your account within agreed limits. It comes in two flavours: [[Sweeping VRP|sweeping]], between your own accounts, and [[Commercial VRP|commercial]], to pay businesses.
x: The grown-up direct debit, with actual limits and an off switch, finally.
g: Automating variable bills and top-ups with limits you actually control.

p: A [[VRP]] that automatically moves money between accounts that all belong to the same person, as opposed to a [[Commercial VRP|commercial]] one that pays third-party businesses.
x: Your money tidying itself between your own pockets while you sleep.
g: Auto-moving your own money between accounts to save or avoid overdrafts.

p: A service that lets a payee send a payment request the payer can accept, decline or defer.
x: The invoice that politely asks, rather than the direct debit that simply takes.
g: Letting billers ask for payment flexibly instead of just taking it.

p: An arrangement where one bank holds accounts and provides services for another to enable [[Cross-Border Payments|cross-border payments]].
x: Banks doing each other favours abroad, slowly, for a fee, since roughly forever.
g: Reaching countries and currencies your own bank cannot touch directly.

p: An account a bank holds locally on behalf of a foreign bank, the mirror image of a nostro.
x: Your money, our shelf. The same account, seen from the other side of the counter.
g: Letting a foreign bank move local currency through a local partner.

p: A [[SWIFT]] service giving [[Cross-Border Payments|cross-border payments]] faster processing, tracking and fee transparency.
x: SWIFT bolting on a tracking number after decades of it will turn up eventually.
g: Tracking a cross-border payment end to end so it stops vanishing.

p: A global messaging standard carrying richer, structured data across payments and securities systems.
x: The industry agreeing, after much pain, on a common language with room for actual detail.
g: Carrying rich payment data that cuts errors and speeds reconciliation.

p: The long-standing international standard for the messages exchanged in card transactions.
x: The ancient grammar every card payment still secretly speaks.
g: The shared format that lets card systems worldwide understand each other.

p: A standardised code identifying a specific bank or branch, better known as a [[SWIFT]] code. Its official ISO name is now the Business Identifier Code, though many still say Bank Identifier Code.
x: A bank's international call sign, eleven characters of this is definitely the right bank.
g: Identifying the exact bank a cross-border payment should reach.

p: A disbursement of funds from a business or platform out to a recipient's account.
x: The lovely direction of money: towards you, for once.
g: Getting money out to sellers, gig workers and partners reliably.

p: A transfer of money, often sent by an individual to family in another country.
x: The quiet, enormous river of money flowing home across borders, taxed by fees the whole way.
g: Sending money home to family abroad, a lifeline for millions.

p: A transaction where payer and payee both bank with the same institution, settled internally.
x: When the money never actually leaves the building, it just changes desks.
g: Settling instantly and cheaply when both sides bank in the same place.

p: A US-dollar [[Stablecoin|stablecoin]] issued by Circle, backed by cash and short-term government debt, the more compliance-minded rival to [[USDT]].
x: The stablecoin that turns up to the regulator's office with its homework done.
g: Moving regulated digital dollars for trading, payouts and settlement.

p: The largest US-dollar [[Stablecoin|stablecoin]] by volume, issued by Tether and heavily used in crypto trading, the higher-liquidity rival to Circle's [[USDC]].
x: The giant of stablecoins, whose reserves the entire internet has loud opinions about.
g: The most liquid way to hold and move dollars across crypto markets.

p: A digital form of a country's official currency issued directly by its central bank. Unlike a [[Stablecoin|stablecoin]], which is issued by a private company and merely backed by reserves, a CBDC is central-bank money itself, a direct claim on the state.
x: Cash, if the central bank could see exactly where every coin went (which is rather the worry). Where a [[Stablecoin|stablecoin]] is a private IOU that swears it is worth a dollar, a CBDC simply is the dollar, issued by the people who make them.
g: Giving people central-bank money in digital form, with new policy controls.

p: Ordinary bank deposits represented as digital tokens on a ledger, still a claim on the bank, unlike a [[Stablecoin|stablecoin]] backed by reserves or a central-bank-issued [[CBDC]].
x: Your bank balance, put on a blockchain, so it can move at the speed everyone now expects.
g: Letting bank money move at [[Blockchain|blockchain]] speed while staying a bank deposit.

p: Finalising a payment directly on a [[Blockchain|blockchain]], recorded and irreversible without a separate clearing step.
x: The money moving and settling in one go, with no three-day wait and no takebacks.
g: Settling value instantly and finally without a separate clearing step.

p: A crypto wallet where a third party holds the private keys and controls the assets for the user.
x: Letting someone else hold your keys. Convenient, until they lose them or you lose them.
g: Easy crypto for people who would rather not manage their own keys.

p: A wallet where only the user holds the private keys and fully controls the assets.
x: Being your own bank, with all the freedom and absolutely none of the customer support.
g: Full control of your crypto with no third party able to freeze it.

p: The fees paid to a [[Blockchain|blockchain]] network to process and confirm a transaction.
x: The toll the blockchain charges to do anything at all, spiking exactly when you are in a hurry.
g: Knowing what a transaction will cost before you hit send, and timing around the spikes.

p: Services that convert traditional money into crypto and crypto back into traditional money.
x: The on-slip and off-slip of the crypto motorway, where most of the fees quietly lurk.
g: Getting money in and out between banks and crypto.

p: Digital money with rules built in, so payments execute automatically when set conditions are met.
x: Money that does what it is told. Wonderful, until it does exactly what it is told.
g: Automating payments that release only when set conditions are met.

p: A transaction where all legs settle at once or none do, removing the risk of paying without receiving, a sharp form of [[Settlement Finality|settlement finality]] most often achieved through [[On-Chain Settlement|on-chain settlement]].
x: All or nothing, simultaneously, so nobody is left holding half a deal.
g: Removing the risk of paying without receiving in a single swap.

p: Self-executing code on a [[Blockchain|blockchain]] that enforces an agreement automatically when conditions are met.
x: A contract that skips the lawyers and just runs, bugs and all.
g: Automating deals and payouts with no middleman to trust or pay.

p: A secondary network on top of a [[Blockchain|blockchain]] that processes transactions faster and cheaper, then settles back.
x: The express lane bolted onto a blockchain that got too popular and too slow.
g: Making blockchain payments fast and cheap enough to actually use.

p: A shared, tamper-resistant ledger spread across many computers that records transactions in linked blocks.
x: A spreadsheet everyone keeps a copy of and nobody can quietly edit. Revolutionary, apparently.
g: A shared record no single party controls, underpinning crypto and tokens.

p: A requirement that crypto firms collect and pass on sender and recipient details for transfers above a threshold.
x: Anti-money-laundering paperwork, finally catching up with the crypto crowd.
g: Keeping crypto transfers traceable enough to fight money laundering.

p: Transactions initiated and completed autonomously by AI agents acting on a person's behalf. The payment layer within the broader [[Agentic Commerce|agentic commerce]].
x: Letting the robot do your shopping, and your paying. What could possibly go wrong.
g: Letting AI assistants buy and pay on your behalf within set limits.

p: Short-term credit that splits a purchase into instalments, often interest-free, paid over a few weeks.
x: Layaway in a hoodie. The old instalment plan, rebranded for people who hate the word credit.
g: Spreading a purchase into instalments, often interest-free, at checkout.

p: Paying a merchant straight from your bank account via [[Open Banking|open banking]], skipping the card networks.
x: The cards' great rival, forever about to take over, perpetually explaining what it is.
g: Cutting card fees by letting customers pay straight from their bank.

p: Payments that happen automatically in the background with no explicit checkout step.
x: Walking out of the shop and being charged later, trusting very hard that the maths was right.
g: Removing checkout friction so paying just happens in the background.

p: A single app bundling messaging, payments, shopping and more into one ecosystem.
x: One app to rule them all, a dream every Western company has and keeps not achieving.
g: Keeping users in one place for chat, shopping and payments together.

p: Mind the overlap. In fintech it means Banking-as-a-Service, licensed banks renting their capabilities to other firms via APIs. In software it often means Backend-as-a-Service, ready-made server infrastructure for app developers.
x: Banking, white-labelled, so your favourite app can pretend it is a bank. Just don't confuse it with the developers' Backend-as-a-Service.
g: Letting a company offer banking features without a licence. Just check whether the speaker means banking or backend.

p: A service letting employees draw wages they have already earned before payday.
x: Payday advances with a fintech haircut and a much friendlier name.
g: Giving workers their already-earned pay before payday hits.

p: Disbursing funds to a recipient in seconds rather than the usual multi-day cycle, whether as [[Open-Loop Payouts|open-loop]] money they can spend anywhere or [[Closed-Loop Payouts|closed-loop]] credit confined to one system.
x: Getting your money now, for a small fee that quietly bets you are impatient.
g: Getting funds to sellers and workers in seconds when they want speed.

p: A US law capping the debit-card [[Interchange|interchange]] fees that large banks can charge merchants.
x: The rare time a regulator looked at card fees and actually said no, less than that.
g: Capping debit fees, saving merchants money on every swipe.

p: The EU's comprehensive crypto framework, covering stablecoins and crypto service providers.
x: Europe putting up the rulebook the crypto party spent years insisting it would never need.
g: Giving crypto firms clear EU rules so they can operate legitimately.

exp: Office of Foreign Assets Control screening
p: Checking customers and transactions against US sanctions lists to block prohibited dealings.
x: The compliance gauntlet making sure your payment is not quietly funding a sanctioned regime.
g: Keeping payments away from sanctioned people, places and entities.

p: Know Your Business: verifying the identity and ownership of a company customer, the corporate cousin of [[KYC]].
x: KYC for companies, which is harder, because companies are very good at hiding who owns them.
g: Verifying the businesses you onboard, not just the individuals.

p: A politically exposed person, in a prominent public role that warrants extra anti-corruption scrutiny.
x: The customer whose government job means the compliance team reads their file very, very closely.
g: Flagging high-risk public figures for the extra scrutiny rules demand.

p: Two things in finance: the confidential report a firm files when it suspects money laundering or financial crime, the domestic counterpart to the international [[STR]] and an output of [[Transaction Monitoring|transaction monitoring]], and, on the markets, the ISO currency code for the Saudi Riyal.
x: One acronym, two finance lives: a quiet note to the regulator about dodgy money, or three letters on a trading screen meaning Saudi Riyal.
g: Alerting authorities to suspected crime, and, on a forex screen, naming the Saudi Riyal.

p: Electronically stored monetary value issued against funds received and accepted as payment, the regulated forebear of its crypto cousin the [[E-Money Token|e-money token]].
x: Money that lives in an app instead of a bank, legally distinct and quietly everywhere.
g: Holding spendable value in an app, the basis of most fintech wallets.

p: Authorisation for an Electronic Money Institution to issue [[E-money|electronic money]] and provide related payment services, the licence behind a great many fintech wallets. (EMI can also mean Equated Monthly Instalment in consumer lending, but in payments it is the institution.)
x: The permit that lets a fintech hold your money and run a wallet without being a full-blown bank.
g: Letting a fintech issue [[E-money|e-money]] and move customer funds without a full banking licence.

p: In payments, the requirement for e-money and payment firms to protect customer funds by segregating them in a [[Safeguarding Account]] or insuring them. In everyday use, safeguarding means protecting children and vulnerable adults from harm, an entirely different field.
x: In fintech, the rule that keeps your money safe if the firm implodes. Everywhere else, a far more serious word about protecting people.
g: Protecting customer funds so they survive if the firm fails, though be aware the word means something very different outside payments.

p: An arrangement where a licensed scheme member lets a fintech issue or acquire cards under its [[BIN|identifier]].
x: A fintech borrowing a real bank's licence to play, with a grown-up co-signing everything.
g: Letting a fintech issue cards by borrowing a licensed bank's access.

p: A US federal law creating a licensing regime for payment-[[Stablecoin|stablecoin]] issuers with strict reserve rules.
x: Congress giving stablecoins a rulebook and, against all odds, a tortured acronym.
g: Giving US stablecoins clear rules, pulling them into the mainstream.

p: A card loaded with funds in advance, spending only the balance already on it.
x: A card with a hard limit, beloved by parents and people who do not trust themselves.
g: Spending a fixed loaded amount, useful for budgets, gifts and kids.

p: A card that exists only as a number, generated digitally for online spending and often spend-controlled.
x: A card with no plastic, conjured for one purchase and often killed straight after.
g: Locking down online spend with single-purpose, controllable numbers. A one-transaction version is the [[Single-Use Card|single-use card]].

p: A [[Virtual Card|virtual card]] number valid for one transaction or merchant, then it expires.
x: A burner card. Used once, discarded, leaving fraudsters nothing to reuse.
g: Killing reuse risk by giving a fraudster a number that dies after one go.

p: A prepaid stored-value card, given or bought to be spent up to its loaded amount.
x: Money with strings attached, spendable in exactly one shop you would not have chosen.
g: Prepaid value to give away or drive store loyalty.

p: A wallet that tokenises your card and passes the payment to the underlying network, staying out of the money flow, unlike the two-step [[Staged Wallet|staged wallet]]. Most [[OEM Wallet|OEM wallets]] work this way.
x: The wallet that just hands your card along, taking a polite step back from the cash itself.
g: Tap-to-pay convenience while the real bank stays in the loop.

p: A wallet that takes money from you, then separately pays the merchant, sitting in the middle in two stages, unlike a [[Pass-Through Wallet|pass-through wallet]] that simply hands the payment to the underlying card.
x: The wallet that stands between you and the shop, holding the money for a beat, like PayPal.
g: Letting people fund a balance once, then spend it anywhere.

p: A payment started by scanning a QR code that encodes the merchant or transaction details.
x: The humble square barcode that conquered payments everywhere except, stubbornly, the West.
g: Cheap, hardware-light payments that scale from market stalls up.

p: Replacing a card's real number on a specific device with a device-bound token, so the real number is never stored.
x: Giving each phone its own disposable stand-in number, useless to anyone who steals it.
g: Letting a phone pay safely without ever storing the real card number.

p: The device-specific token number that stands in for the real card number, or [[FPAN]], inside a wallet, a kind of [[Network Tokens|network token]] bound to a single handset.
x: The alias your phone uses so the shop never learns your card's real name.
g: The device-bound number that keeps wallet payments useless to thieves.

p: Phishing-resistant cryptographic credentials that replace passwords, increasingly used to authorise payments.
x: The long-promised death of the password, arriving one reluctant login at a time.
g: Killing passwords and the phishing and fraud that come with them.

p: Offsetting mutual obligations so only the net difference is settled, rather than every gross amount, the arithmetic behind [[Net Settlement|net settlement]] and its delayed form, [[Deferred Net Settlement|deferred net settlement]].
x: Two parties cancelling out who owes what, then settling only the awkward remainder.
g: Slashing how much money actually has to move between counterparties.

p: Settling only the netted balances of many transactions at set times, rather than one by one as [[RTGS]] does. Run on a delay it becomes [[Deferred Net Settlement|deferred net settlement]], and the offsetting itself is [[Netting|netting]].
x: Tallying up the whole day's back-and-forth and moving just the difference at closing.
g: Settling efficiently by moving only the day's net differences.

p: A central institution that processes, nets and helps settle transactions among its members.
x: The great sorting office of payments, deciding who owes whom before anyone pays.
g: Centralising clearing so members do not settle one-to-one with everyone.

p: The risk that one party fails to deliver its side of a deal after the other has already paid, including the cross-currency variety known as [[Herstatt Risk|Herstatt risk]], and largely tamed by [[Settlement Finality|settlement finality]].
x: The quiet terror that you have paid and the other side has just gone very quiet.
g: Helping firms measure and contain the danger of paying before they are paid.

p: The cross-currency form of [[Settlement Risk|settlement risk]] where you pay one leg of an [[FX]] trade and the counterparty collapses before paying the other.
x: Named after a 1974 bank failure: the nightmare of paying out at dawn to a counterparty dead by lunch.
g: Helping risk teams design FX settlement that can't leave one side paid and the other not.

p: The date funds actually become available in an account, distinct from the transaction date.
x: The day the money is really yours, as opposed to the day the screen optimistically said so.
g: Knowing when money is genuinely usable, not just showing on screen.

p: A portion of a merchant's sales withheld for a period to cover potential chargebacks or refunds.
x: The processor keeping a slice of your takings, just in case your customers come asking for it back.
g: Protecting a processor against a merchant's future chargebacks.

p: A sub-account number tied to a real bank account, used to attribute and reconcile incoming payments.
x: A fistful of fake account numbers pointing at one real one, so you can tell who actually paid.
g: Telling instantly who paid you by giving each payer their own number. The [[IBAN]]-format version is the [[Virtual IBAN]].

p: An arrangement where a neutral third party holds funds until agreed conditions are met.
x: The trusted referee holding the money so neither side has to trust the other.
g: Letting strangers transact safely with funds held until both deliver.

p: Placing funds into an account in advance so payments can be made when they fall due.
x: Parking money somewhere ahead of time so the rail does not stall mid-payment.
g: Keeping money pre-positioned so payouts never stall for lack of funds.

p: A person who lets criminals move stolen or laundered money through their own bank account, knowingly or not, often as one hop in a chain that feeds a [[Funnel Account|funnel account]].
x: The unwitting, or witting, middleman whose account becomes a launderette for someone else's dirty cash.
g: Helping banks and police spot the accounts that quietly funnel scam proceeds onward.

p: A scam in which the victim is tricked into authorising a real payment to a fraudster, so the bank sees a legitimate transfer, the very loophole a [[Confirmation of Payee|Confirmation of Payee]] check aims to close and that [[APP Reimbursement|APP reimbursement]] rules try to make good after the fact.
x: The cruellest kind of fraud: you press send yourself, having been very convincingly lied to.
g: Naming the fraud that beats normal defences, because the genuine customer approved it.

p: The UK requirement, in force since 2024, for banks to refund most victims of authorised [[Push Payment|push payment]] scams, the [[APP Scam|APP scams]] where the customer is tricked into paying, with the cost shared between the sending and receiving banks.
x: The rule that finally stopped banks shrugging at scam victims and saying 'you did press send, technically'.
g: Getting scam victims their money back, and giving banks a sharp reason to stop the fraud.

p: A UK check that confirms the name on a bank account matches the intended payee before a transfer is made, the EU equivalent being [[Verification of Payee]], introduced largely to blunt the [[APP Scam|APP scams]] that rely on sending money to the wrong name.
x: The system that finally asks 'are you sure that is who you think it is' before your deposit vanishes.
g: Cutting misdirected payments and scams by catching wrong names before you send.

p: Fraud carried out using [[Push Payment|push payments]], where money is sent out instantly and is very hard to recall once it has gone, the mechanism behind the typical [[APP Scam|APP scam]].
x: Fraud that loves instant rails, because by the time you realise, the money has already left the building.
g: Naming the fast-rail fraud that drives demand for name checks and reimbursement rules.

p: Checking customers and payments against government sanctions lists to block dealings with banned people, entities and countries.
x: The global compliance net making sure your transfer is not quietly funding someone the world has blacklisted.
g: Keeping firms out of legal disaster by stopping payments to sanctioned parties.

p: The ongoing, automated screening of payments for patterns that suggest money laundering, fraud or other crime, the after-the-fact counterpart to real-time [[Transaction Screening|transaction screening]].
x: The tireless system reading every transaction for anything that smells faintly of wrongdoing.
g: Catching suspicious activity in the flow, the engine behind most [[AML]] and fraud alerts.

p: A scam where fraudsters hijack or spoof a business email account to trick staff into sending payments or changing bank details, often through [[Invoice Redirection|invoice redirection]], and a corporate cousin of the broader [[APP Scam]].
x: The fake email from the boss, the supplier or the lawyer, asking ever so urgently for a quick transfer.
g: Naming the corporate scam that drains finance teams who thought they were just doing their job.

p: A scam that swaps the bank details on a genuine invoice so payment lands with the fraudster instead of the real supplier, a form of [[APP Scam|APP scam]] often set up through a [[BEC|business email compromise]].
x: The same invoice you were expecting, with the account number quietly switched to a stranger's.
g: Flagging the scam that turns routine supplier payments into a costly mistake.

p: A fraud where criminals hijack your mobile number to intercept the one-time codes that protect your accounts, a common route to [[Account Takeover|account takeover]] and, from there, to an [[APP Scam|APP scam]].
x: When a stranger talks your network into handing over your phone number, and with it, your text-message security.
g: Explaining why SMS codes are weaker than they look, and why passkeys are winning.

p: Manipulating people, rather than hacking systems, into handing over money, credentials or access.
x: Hacking the human instead of the computer, usually with a convincing story and a sense of urgency.
g: Naming the root technique behind most scams, which no firewall can patch.

p: Identifying a device by its unique combination of settings and signals, to spot fraud and recognise returning users.
x: Recognising your phone or laptop by its quirks, like a bouncer who never forgets a face.
g: Catching fraudsters reusing devices, and smoothing checkout for genuine repeat customers.

p: Asking for extra proof of identity only when a payment or login looks risky enough to warrant it, the action [[Risk-Based Authentication|risk-based authentication]] triggers and a way to satisfy [[SCA]] without challenging everyone.
x: The security that stays out of your way until something looks off, then suddenly wants your fingerprint.
g: Balancing fraud control and friction by challenging only the risky cases.

p: Deciding how much authentication to demand based on the assessed risk of each transaction or login, the principle behind [[Step-Up Authentication|step-up authentication]], which adds extra proof only when the risk warrants it.
x: Security that reads the room: a wave-through for the obvious, a grilling for the suspicious.
g: Cutting friction for safe payments while still catching the dodgy ones.

p: A scam where the fraudster pretends to be someone you trust, such as your bank, the police or a relative, to extract a payment, one of the most common forms of [[APP Scam|APP scam]].
x: The call from your bank's fraud team that is, ironically, the fraud.
g: Naming the trust-based scam that no amount of card security can stop.

p: A scam luring victims into fake investments, often crypto or shares, promising returns that never arrive, a flavour of [[APP Scam|APP scam]] and the endgame of [[Pig Butchering|pig butchering]].
x: The too-good-to-be-true opportunity that was, reliably, too good to be true.
g: Flagging the scam category that costs victims the most, one fake portfolio at a time.

p: A scam where a buyer pays for goods or services that are never delivered, common on marketplaces and social media, and a leading form of [[APP Scam]].
x: The bargain that arrives as a read receipt and then silence.
g: Naming the everyday scam behind a huge share of authorised [[Push Payment|push payment]] losses.

p: In [[Open Banking]], the bank or provider that holds the customer's account and must give licensed third parties access to it, the account-holding side that any [[Third-Party Provider|third-party provider]] plugs into.
x: The bank on the other side of open banking, legally obliged to open the door when you say so.
g: Naming the account-holding side that PISPs and AISPs plug into.

p: A case where [[SCA|strong customer authentication]] can be skipped, such as low-value or low-risk payments, to reduce checkout friction.
x: The official permission to not make the customer jump through the security hoop this time.
g: Letting merchants smooth checkout without breaking the authentication rules.

p: Variable recurring payments, often shortened to cVRP, used to pay third-party businesses such as utilities or subscriptions, rather than the [[Sweeping VRP|sweeping]] kind that just moves your own money. See [[VRP]].
x: The open-banking answer to the direct debit, finally pointed at paying companies, not just yourself.
g: Letting people authorise flexible, capped recurring payments to billers straight from their bank. The EU equivalent is [[DRP|dynamic recurring payments]].

p: An open-banking payment journey where the user is handed straight into their banking app to approve, then back to the merchant.
x: The smooth open-banking hop into your bank app and back, with no passwords typed.
g: Lifting open-banking conversion by skipping clunky logins for a quick app approval.

p: An authentication journey that sends the user to their bank's website or app to approve, then returns them to the merchant.
x: The checkout detour to your bank and back, hopefully without losing you along the way.
g: Letting banks authenticate payments on their own turf, at some cost to conversion.

p: An EU law, applying from 2025, requiring financial firms to withstand, respond to and recover from IT disruptions and cyberattacks.
x: Brussels telling finance that falling over when the systems break is no longer an acceptable plan.
g: Forcing payment and finance firms to take [[Operational Resilience|operational resilience]] seriously, on pain of penalty.

p: A firm's ability to keep critical services running, and recover quickly, through disruptions like outages and cyberattacks.
x: The grown-up acceptance that things will break, and an actual plan for when they inevitably do.
g: Keeping payments flowing through outages, now a hard regulatory expectation.

p: The UK regulator that authorises and supervises payment firms, banks and most of the financial sector.
x: The UK watchdog whose approval fintechs crave and whose letters they dread.
g: Licensing and policing UK payment firms, the gatekeeper to operating at all.

exp: Payment Systems Regulator / Payment Services Regulation
p: Confusingly, up to three things. In the UK, the Payment Systems Regulator (a watchdog) and the Payment Services Regulations 2017 (the rulebook). In the EU, the Payment Services Regulation that accompanies [[PSD3]].
x: One acronym, three regulators' worth of confusion: a UK watchdog, a UK rulebook, or an EU regulation, depending entirely who is talking.
g: Flagging either the UK regulator or the EU regulation. Just be sure which side of the Channel you mean.

p: The real person who ultimately owns or controls a company, the human that business onboarding checks are trying to find.
x: The actual person behind the shell, the layers and the nominee directors. Compliance's favourite hide-and-seek.
g: Stopping criminals from hiding behind corporate structures, the goal of know-your-business checks.

p: The baseline identity and risk checks a firm runs on a customer to meet anti-money-laundering rules, the operational heart of [[KYC]] and the starting point that escalates to [[EDD]] when the risk is higher.
x: The homework every regulated firm must do before it will let you near its services.
g: Verifying who a customer is and how risky they are, the foundation of [[AML]].

p: Measures to stop money reaching terrorist groups, almost always paired with anti-money-laundering controls.
x: The grimmer twin of AML, watching for money headed somewhere genuinely dangerous.
g: Keeping payment systems from being used to fund terrorism, a legal must for every firm.

p: An EU law requiring banks to offer instant euro payments at no extra cost, with name checks, across the bloc.
x: Brussels deciding that instant, free euro transfers should be a right, not a premium feature.
g: Forcing euro instant payments to be universal, cheap and safer.

p: The total value of goods or services sold through a platform over a period, before fees and refunds.
x: The big headline number a marketplace loves to quote, conveniently before all the deductions.
g: Sizing a platform's total sales, the top-line metric investors fixate on.

p: The total value of payments a provider processes over a period, a core scale metric for payment companies.
x: The number a payments firm flexes to prove it matters, measured in eye-watering billions.
g: Measuring how much money a provider moves, the headline of every fintech pitch deck.

p: The share of payment or transaction value a provider keeps as revenue, usually a small percentage.
x: The thin slice a platform skims off every transaction, which adds up to a very thick fortune.
g: Showing how a payments business actually makes money, one fraction of a percent at a time.

p: The margin a provider adds to the mid-market exchange rate when converting currency, often the real cost of a transfer.
x: The quiet padding on the exchange rate, where the fee you were told was 'free' actually hides.
g: Revealing the true cost of a currency conversion, so you can compare providers honestly.

p: Any way to pay that is not a traditional card, from bank transfers and wallets to [[BNPL]] and local schemes.
x: The industry's catch-all shrug for everything that is not a Visa or a Mastercard.
g: Naming the fast-growing non-card methods merchants increasingly have to accept.

p: A payment method popular in a specific country or region, such as [[iDEAL]] in the Netherlands or Pix in Brazil.
x: The way a whole country prefers to pay, which your global checkout ignores at its peril.
g: Reminding global merchants that the world does not, in fact, all pay by card.

p: The formal, regulation-friendly term for digital assets like cryptocurrencies and tokens, used in laws such as the EU's [[MiCA]].
x: What regulators call crypto when they want to sound serious and write rules about it.
g: Giving lawmakers a precise term to regulate the sprawling world of digital tokens.

p: The cash and low-risk holdings a [[Stablecoin|stablecoin]] issuer keeps to back its tokens one-for-one.
x: The real money supposedly sitting behind every stablecoin, which everyone very much hopes is actually there.
g: Underpinning a stablecoin's promise that one token is really worth one dollar.

p: Government-issued currency like pounds, euros or dollars, which holds value because the state and its users say so, not because it is backed by gold.
x: Normal money. The boring, trusted, state-backed stuff that crypto keeps promising to replace and mostly has not.
g: The anchor of the whole system, the thing crypto is priced against and forever converts back into.

p: A card a merchant keeps on file with the cardholder's agreement, for future or recurring payments.
x: Your card, parked with a merchant for next time, which is either wonderfully convenient or quietly alarming.
g: Letting returning customers and subscriptions pay without re-entering a card.

p: A payment a merchant triggers using a stored card, without the customer present, such as a subscription renewal.
x: The charge that happens while you are asleep, because you agreed to it several months ago.
g: Powering subscriptions and automatic top-ups from a card already on file.

p: The text that identifies a purchase on a cardholder's bank or card statement.
x: The cryptic line on your statement that makes you briefly question whether you bought that at all.
g: Helping customers recognise charges, and cutting the disputes caused by mystery line items.
