p: A card decline that should not be retried, such as a lost, stolen or closed card.
x: The bank's firm and final no, the kind that retrying will only make more embarrassing.
g: Telling merchants when to stop trying, sparing pointless retries and fees.

p: The [[Reason Code|reason code]] an issuer returns when it refuses a transaction, indicating why it was declined.
x: The bank's terse explanation for the rejection, ranging from 'no funds' to 'we would rather not say'.
g: Telling merchants why a payment failed, and whether it is worth trying again.

p: A checkout page hosted by the payment provider, so card data never touches the merchant's own systems.
x: Outsourcing the dangerous bit of checkout to someone who actually wants the security paperwork.
g: Taking cards online while keeping card data, and [[PCI Scope|PCI scope]], off your servers. Compare the open-banking [[Embedded Flow|embedded flow]], where the equivalent sensitive step happens inside the provider's own interface instead.

p: Handling a payment or workflow automatically from start to finish, with no manual intervention.
x: The dream where payments process themselves and no human ever has to touch the spreadsheet.
g: The benchmark for efficient payment operations, and the goal of every automation project.

p: The point at which a payment becomes legally irrevocable and cannot be reversed or unwound, the certainty that contains [[Settlement Risk|settlement risk]] and that [[Atomic Settlement|atomic settlement]] delivers in a single all-or-nothing swap.
x: The moment the money is well and truly gone, with no backsies, legally guaranteed.
g: Giving everyone certainty that a settled payment will stay settled, even if a bank fails.

p: The funds a bank needs available during the day to meet its payment obligations as they fall due.
x: The cash a bank must keep sloshing about so it can pay its bills before close of play.
g: Keeping high-value payment systems flowing without anyone running dry mid-afternoon.

p: The standard timeframe over which a trade or payment settles, such as T+1 or T+2 days after the transaction, within which the [[Settlement Window|settlement window]] does the actual moving.
x: The industry's polite way of saying the money will get there eventually, on a schedule.
g: Setting expectations for when funds actually move, and shrinking over time.

p: Where payments that fail automated processing wait for a human to fix missing or incorrect details.
x: The naughty step for broken payments, staffed by ops teams correcting other people's typos.
g: Catching and fixing malformed payments before they bounce or go astray.

p: Infrastructure that routes each transaction to the right network, processor or rail, the engine behind [[Smart Routing]] and a core part of any [[Payment Hub]].
x: The traffic cop of payments, pointing every transaction down the correct road.
g: Routing transactions reliably across many networks from a single connection.

p: A central technology platform that consolidates a bank's or enterprise's many payment types and rails into one processing system. Not to be confused with a [[Payment Factory|payment factory]], the corporate-treasury model that often sits on top of it.
x: The grand unifying project every big bank attempts, to replace forty creaking systems with one.
g: Letting an enterprise run all its payment flows through a single, modern core.

p: Automatically sending each transaction down the path most likely to succeed or to cost the least.
x: Payments picking their own optimal route, like a satnav avoiding the toll road and the traffic.
g: Lifting approval rates and cutting costs by choosing the best path for every payment.

p: The dominant online bank-transfer payment method in the Netherlands, used for most Dutch e-commerce.
x: How the Netherlands pays online, to the bafflement of anyone expecting a card field.
g: Reaching Dutch shoppers, who overwhelmingly prefer paying straight from their bank.

p: A pan-European wallet and instant-payment service from the European Payments Initiative, built to rival cards and big-tech wallets. Built by the [[EPI]] consortium.
x: Europe's latest attempt to build a homegrown wallet and stop leaning on American card networks.
g: Giving Europe an instant, account-based way to pay across borders, if it catches on.

p: Singapore's instant payment service, letting people pay using a mobile number or registration ID.
x: Singapore's tidy, instant way to pay a person or shop with just a phone number.
g: Moving money instantly in Singapore with no account numbers or cards.

p: Thailand's instant payment system, linking transfers to national ID or mobile numbers.
x: Thailand's instant rail that made QR codes and phone-number payments the everyday norm.
g: Powering instant, low-cost payments across Thailand, cash's great challenger.

p: Payment accounts held on a mobile phone rather than at a bank, hugely important across Africa and parts of Asia, with M-Pesa the best-known example.
x: Banking by phone for the unbanked, which leapfrogged cards and branches entirely.
g: Bringing payments to billions without bank accounts, via a simple mobile balance.

p: Australia's [[Real-Time Payments|real-time payments]] infrastructure, underpinning instant transfers and services like [[PayTo]] and [[PayID]].
x: The Australian rail doing the instant heavy lifting behind the friendlier brand names.
g: Settling Australian payments in real time, the backbone of [[Osko]], [[PayTo]] and [[PayID]].

p: Australia's addressing service that lets people receive payments using an alias like a phone number or email instead of account details, the addressing layer on the [[NPP]] alongside [[PayTo]] for recurring debits.
x: The Australian trick of being paid via your email, so you never read out a BSB again.
g: Making instant Australian payments simpler by hiding the account numbers.

p: An [[IBAN]]-format [[Virtual Account|virtual account]] number that points to an underlying real account, used to attribute and route incoming payments.
x: A throwaway account number that looks real and quietly forwards the money to where it actually lives.
g: Telling businesses exactly who paid by giving each customer their own IBAN.

p: A separate account where an e-money or payment firm holds customer funds apart from its own money, the mechanism behind [[Safeguarding]].
x: The ring-fenced pot that is supposed to keep your money safe if your fintech goes under.
g: Protecting customer funds by keeping them legally separate from the firm's own cash.

p: A licensed bank that lets a fintech access payment schemes or hold funds under the bank's own permissions.
x: The grown-up bank that co-signs so a fintech can play, taking a cut and the regulatory worry.
g: Letting fintechs reach the rails and schemes without a full banking licence of their own.

p: A firm authorised to provide payment services, such as transfers and acquiring, without issuing electronic money or being a bank.
x: A regulated payments company that is not quite a bank and definitely not an e-money issuer, but something in between.
g: Letting a firm offer payment services under a lighter licence than a full bank.

p: Dividing a single payment across multiple recipients, such as a marketplace paying several sellers from one checkout.
x: One payment, neatly carved up between everyone owed a slice, automatically.
g: Letting marketplaces and platforms pay multiple parties from a single transaction.

p: The open-banking service of accessing a customer's account data, with their consent, to display or analyse it. Provided by an [[AISP]], it is the read-only twin of payment-moving [[PIS]].
x: The 'let me see your accounts' half of open banking, as opposed to the 'let me move your money' half.
g: Powering budgeting, accounting and lending apps that read your bank data with permission.

p: The open-banking service of initiating a payment directly from a customer's bank account, with their consent. Provided by a [[PISP]], it is the money-moving twin of read-only [[AIS]].
x: The 'let me move your money' half of open banking, the bit that actually pushes the payment.
g: Letting apps trigger account-to-account payments without ever touching a card.

p: The third open-banking provider type, alongside the [[AISP]] and the [[PISP]]: a card issuer that, with consent, checks whether funds are available in another bank's account before a card payment.
x: The forgotten third sibling of open banking, quietly asking your bank 'do they have the money' before a card goes through.
g: Letting card issuers confirm funds are there without seeing your full balance.

p: Disbursing funds to recipients over open networks like cards or bank transfer, so the money can be spent anywhere, unlike [[Closed-Loop Payouts|closed-loop]] credit confined to one system. Often delivered as [[Instant Payouts|instant payouts]]. The same distinction applies to instruments, not just payouts: an open-loop instrument runs on the open networks and is accepted anywhere.
x: Paying people out in real, spend-anywhere money, not points stuck inside your own walled garden.
g: Getting funds to gig workers, sellers and customers in money they can actually use anywhere.

p: A stored bank-payment authorisation that lets a customer [[Pay by Bank|pay by bank]] again without re-authorising each time, the account-to-account answer to [[Card-on-File|card-on-file]].
x: Your bank details saved for next time, so paying by bank stops being a one-night stand.
g: Letting open-banking and direct payments become as repeatable as a saved card.

p: The UK framework extending [[Open Banking|open-banking]]-style data sharing to sectors like energy, telecoms and retail, letting people share their data to unlock better deals and services.
x: Open banking's ambitious sequel, applying the same 'share your data, get a better deal' idea to your gas bill and your broadband.
g: Promising easier switching and smarter services right across the economy, not just your bank. Where [[Open Finance]] extends data-sharing across the rest of finance, Smart Data takes it beyond finance entirely.

p: The nine biggest UK banks and building societies that the Competition and Markets Authority ordered to kick-start [[Open Banking|open banking]] by opening up their data.
x: The nine incumbents dragged, not entirely willingly, into building open banking because the regulator made them.
g: Naming the banks whose forced participation bootstrapped UK open banking.

p: The regulatory term for the end user of a payment service, whether they are the payer, the payee, or both.
x: You, in the eyes of a payments lawyer: not a person, but a Payment Services User.
g: Giving regulation a tidy label for the human at the end of every payment.

p: A company that supplies the technology behind regulated open-banking providers, without being regulated itself, the open-banking parallel to a card-world [[Token Requestor|token requestor]] plumbing in [[Network Tokens|network tokens]].
x: The behind-the-scenes tech vendor doing the heavy lifting while the regulated firm takes the credit and the liability.
g: Letting fintechs offer [[Open Banking|open banking]] without building all the plumbing themselves.

p: The detailed technical rules, written by the European Banking Authority, that spell out how laws like [[PSD2]] must actually be implemented, covering security, [[SCA]] and [[API]] access.
x: The fine print beneath the regulation, where 'be secure' becomes three hundred pages of exactly how.
g: Turning broad payment law into the precise rules firms must build to.

p: Disbursing funds within a single closed system, such as store credit or a platform balance, that can only be spent inside that system, unlike [[Open-Loop Payouts|open-loop]] money that lands as spend-anywhere funds. The same distinction applies to instruments, not just payouts: a closed-loop instrument, like store credit or a transit card, only works inside one system.
x: Paying people in points, credit or a wallet balance that only works in your shop, never the wider world.
g: Keeping value and customers inside your own ecosystem, the opposite of [[Open-Loop Payouts|open-loop]].

p: The issuer-side server that actually runs a [[3-D Secure]] check, deciding whether to wave a payment through or throw up a [[Challenge Flow|challenge]], reached via the scheme's [[Directory Server|directory server]] after the merchant's [[3DS Server|3DS Server]] kicks things off.
x: The bouncer's clipboard at your bank, quietly judging whether your online payment looks like you.
g: Letting your bank make the final authentication call on a card-not-present payment.

p: The card scheme's switchboard in [[3-D Secure]], routing authentication messages between the merchant's [[3DS Server]] and the issuer's [[ACS]].
x: The middle child of 3DS: it doesn't decide anything, it just makes sure the other two are talking.
g: Connecting merchant and issuer so an authentication request reaches the right bank.

p: The merchant or acquirer-side component that kicks off a [[3-D Secure]] check by bundling up transaction and device data and sending it to the [[Directory Server|directory server]], which routes it on to the issuer's [[ACS]].
x: The keen intern who starts the whole 3DS conversation, gathers the paperwork and passes it up the chain.
g: Starting authentication from the merchant's side so a payment can earn its [[Liability Shift|liability shift]].

p: The cryptographic stamp produced when a [[3-D Secure]] check passes, slipped into the [[Authorisation|authorisation]] so the issuer knows authentication really happened, and read alongside the [[ECI]] that signals how strongly the payment was authenticated.
x: 3DS's little receipt proving you passed the test, without which the [[Liability Shift|liability]] doesn't move.
g: Carrying proof of authentication into the payment so liability shifts to the issuer.

p: A code attached to an online card payment showing whether and how strongly it was authenticated via [[3-D Secure]], and therefore who carries the [[Liability Shift|fraud liability]], the companion to the cryptographic [[CAVV]] that proves the check happened.
x: A one-digit gossip note on every online payment, telling the issuer exactly how much the merchant bothered to check.
g: Signalling the authentication outcome so everyone knows who is on the hook for fraud.

p: A [[3-D Secure]] mode where you approve the payment somewhere separate, like your banking app, rather than inside the checkout itself.
x: When the shop pauses while your bank app buzzes 'was this you?' on a totally different screen.
g: Letting you authenticate out of band when a redirect or pop-up would be clunky. The open-banking analogue is the [[Decoupled Flow|decoupled flow]].

p: When the card scheme approves or declines a transaction on the issuer's behalf because the issuer's own systems are unreachable.
x: The scheme covering for your bank when it nods off, making the yes-or-no call so the checkout doesn't just hang.
g: Keeping payments flowing when an issuer's systems are down or too slow.

p: When a chip read fails and the terminal drops back to the less-secure [[Magnetic Stripe|magnetic stripe]] to get the payment done.
x: The card machine giving up on the chip and shrugging 'fine, swipe it', much to a fraud team's dismay.
g: Salvaging a sale when the chip won't read, at the cost of weaker security.

p: A [[Authorisation|authorisation]] for no money at all, used purely to check a card is valid and active without charging it.
x: Knocking on the card's door to see if anyone's home, then leaving without taking a penny.
g: Verifying a card before storing it or starting a subscription, with no charge.

p: How a cardholder proves it is really them at the [[Point of Sale|point of sale]], by [[Chip and PIN|PIN]], signature, biometric or nothing at all, with a CVM Limit setting the value above which proof is required.
x: The terminal's way of asking 'and you are?', and the price ceiling under which it can't be bothered to ask.
g: Deciding when a shopper must actively prove themselves versus just tapping and going.

p: The vague, catch-all issuer [[Decline|decline]] that gives no real reason, just a flat refusal to approve the payment.
x: The 'computer says no' of card declines: maximally unhelpful, infuriatingly common.
g: Technically nothing, but it's the decline merchants most want to decode and recover.

p: A scheme identifier that ties every [[Network Tokens|token]] and the underlying [[PAN]] back to one account, without ever exposing the real card number.
x: A secret backstage pass linking all your card's disguises to one identity, so fraud teams can join the dots safely.
g: Recognising the same underlying card across tokens and devices without seeing the [[PAN]].

p: A payment the cardholder is actively present for and triggers themselves, the counterpart to a [[MIT|Merchant-Initiated Transaction]].
x: You, actually there, tapping 'pay now', as opposed to a subscription quietly helping itself later.
g: Distinguishing the payments you start from the ones a merchant runs on your behalf.

p: The identifier from a card's first [[Authorisation|authorisation]] that must be chained onto every later [[MIT|merchant-initiated]] charge to prove they belong together.
x: The thread of breadcrumbs linking your first payment to every subscription charge after it.
g: Linking stored-credential payments so issuers approve recurring charges rather than declining them.

p: The acquirer and merchant's formal re-submission of a disputed transaction, with evidence, to fight a [[Chargeback|chargeback]].
x: The merchant's 'actually, hold on' in the chargeback boxing match, evidence in hand.
g: Giving merchants a structured way to contest a chargeback they believe is wrong.

p: A late stage in a card [[Dispute|dispute]] where one side asks the other to concede before it escalates to a formal, binding ruling by the card scheme.
x: The 'are you really sure you want to take this to the judge?' round, just before things get expensive.
g: Settling disputes before the costly final ruling, saving both sides fees.

p: The standardised code a card scheme attaches to a [[Chargeback|chargeback]] to explain why it was raised, from fraud to goods-not-received.
x: The reason-for-divorce field on a chargeback, picked from the scheme's menu of grievances.
g: Telling the merchant exactly what they're accused of, so they know how to respond.

exp: Member Alert to Control High-risk Merchants list
p: Mastercard's shared blacklist of merchants terminated for cause, screened by acquirers during [[Underwriting|onboarding]].
x: The payments industry's naughty step: land on it and good luck getting another merchant account.
g: Warning acquirers off merchants that were booted elsewhere for fraud or excessive [[Chargeback|chargebacks]].

p: Real-time warnings (via networks like Ethoca and Verifi) that tip a merchant off to a brewing [[Dispute|dispute]] so they can refund before it becomes a [[Chargeback|chargeback]].
x: An early-warning siren letting you defuse a chargeback by refunding first and saving the black mark.
g: Heading off chargebacks, and their [[Chargeback Ratio|ratio]] damage, by resolving disputes pre-emptively.

p: A card carrying two scheme brands at once, such as a [[Domestic Scheme|domestic scheme]] plus Visa or Mastercard, so a payment can be routed either way.
x: A card with two passports, letting the merchant (or regulator) pick the cheaper border to cross.
g: Enabling cheaper domestic routing while keeping global acceptance as a backup. Not to be confused with a [[Co-Brand Card|co-brand card]], which is about retail loyalty branding, not routing.

p: A card jointly branded by a scheme and a retailer, airline or other partner, usually bundling loyalty perks with the card.
x: The airline card that earns you miles and a faint sense you're spending more than you meant to.
g: Tying a scheme card to a brand's loyalty programme to drive spend and stickiness. Not to be confused with a [[Co-Badged Card|co-badged card]], which carries two payment schemes, not a retail partner.

p: A national card network, like Cartes Bancaires, [[RuPay]], Mir or Elo, operating alongside the global brands within its home market.
x: The local card hero that quietly handles most of a country's payments while Visa and Mastercard get the posters.
g: Keeping card payments cheap and sovereign within a single country.

p: A corporate card built for company procurement spend, often eligible for cheaper [[Interchange|interchange]] when rich [[Level 2 & Level 3 Data|line-item data]] is supplied.
x: The card the office uses to buy staplers in bulk, with a spreadsheet attached to every swipe.
g: Streamlining business buying and cutting card costs through enhanced data.

p: A restricted-use corporate card for vehicle fuel and maintenance, usually with controls limiting where and what it can buy.
x: The card that only believes in petrol and oil changes, and politely declines your lunch.
g: Controlling and tracking fuel and vehicle spend across a company's drivers.

p: A certified tamper-resistant chip inside a phone or card that safely stores wallet credentials and runs the sensitive bits of a payment, the hardware counterpart to software-based [[HCE]], and the safe vault that [[Push Provisioning|push provisioning]] loads cards into.
x: The tiny bank vault soldered into your phone, where your card's secrets actually live.
g: Keeping tokenised card data safe on a device so [[OEM Wallet|wallet]] payments can't be skimmed.

p: Software-based card emulation that lets an Android phone make [[Contactless|contactless]] payments without relying on a hardware [[Secure Element|secure element]], using the cloud instead.
x: Faking a chip in software so your phone can tap to pay without a dedicated vault on board.
g: Letting banks launch tap-to-pay wallets without wrangling handset hardware.

p: Adding a card to an [[OEM Wallet|mobile wallet]] straight from the issuer's banking app, rather than typing the number into the wallet yourself, with the card stored as a [[DPAN|device token]].
x: Your bank app firing the card into Apple or Google Wallet for you, no squinting at the long number required.
g: Making it one tap to load a freshly issued card into a wallet, lifting activation.

p: The real underlying card number that a wallet's [[DPAN|device token]] maps back to when it's time to actually fund the payment, in other words the cardholder's true [[PAN]].
x: The card's true name, hidden behind the alias your phone shows the shop.
g: Linking a device token back to the genuine account that pays the bill.

p: The registered entity, such as Apple, Google or a merchant, that asks the [[TSP|token service]] to issue a [[Network Tokens|network token]] for a card.
x: Whoever puts their hand up to say 'I'd like a disposable version of this card, please'.
g: Identifying who is allowed to request and use tokens for a given card.

p: EMVCo's standard for storing and tokenising card credentials for online checkout, the engine humming beneath [[Click to Pay]].
x: The industry's attempt at one tidy card-on-file standard, so every site stops reinventing the checkout.
g: Powering a consistent, tokenised [[Guest Checkout|guest checkout]] across merchants and schemes.

p: A device-maker's wallet, like Apple Pay, Google Pay or Samsung Wallet, tied to the handset's [[Secure Element|secure element]], as opposed to a bank or scheme wallet. It is typically a [[Pass-Through Wallet|pass-through wallet]], loaded in one tap via [[Push Provisioning|push provisioning]].
x: The wallet that came baked into your phone whether you asked for it or not.
g: Turning the phone in your pocket into a [[Contactless|contactless]] card.

p: Securely storing a tokenised payment credential so it can be reused for one-click or recurring payments without re-entering details.
x: Putting your card in the shop's safe so next time you just wave it through.
g: Underpinning [[Card-on-File|saved cards]], [[One-Click Checkout|one-click]] and subscriptions.

p: A full checkout flow hosted by the payment provider but rendered inline within the merchant's own page, rather than a redirect or pop-up.
x: The checkout that feels like it's the shop's own, even though someone else is doing the risky bit.
g: Keeping shoppers on-site while offloading [[PCI DSS|PCI]] burden to the provider. Distinct from the open-banking [[Embedded Flow|embedded flow]], which governs where you enter bank credentials, not the checkout UI.

p: Completing a purchase with stored credentials in a single tap, no re-entering of card or address details.
x: The dangerously frictionless button that turns 'just browsing' into 'it's arriving Tuesday'.
g: Slashing [[Cart Abandonment|abandonment]] for returning shoppers.

p: Letting a shopper pay without creating an account, the counterpart to an account or returning-customer checkout.
x: The 'no thanks, I don't want a relationship' option that quietly saves a lot of sales.
g: Cutting friction and [[Cart Abandonment|abandonment]] for first-time buyers.

p: Returning part of a captured payment rather than the whole amount, for instance refunding one item from a larger order.
x: Giving back some, but not all, of the money, because only the left shoe was faulty.
g: Handling partial returns and price adjustments without unwinding the whole sale.

p: The first stage of money laundering: getting dirty cash into the financial system in the first place, often via deposits, shops or [[Funnel Account|funnel accounts]], before [[Layering|layering]] and [[Integration|integration]] follow.
x: Step one of laundering: smuggling the grubby money through the front door before anyone asks where it came from.
g: Nothing good, but naming the stage helps banks spot it. The riskiest moment for a launderer to be caught.

p: The second stage of money laundering, after [[Placement|placement]] and before [[Integration|integration]]: shuffling funds through layers of transactions, accounts and jurisdictions to blur their origin.
x: The 'now you see it, now you don't' shell game that buries where the money actually came from.
g: Again, nothing legitimate, but understanding it is how investigators unpick a laundering chain.

p: The final stage of money laundering, after [[Placement|placement]] and [[Layering|layering]], bringing the cleaned funds back as seemingly legitimate wealth, like property or business income.
x: The grand finale where dirty money strolls out in a nice suit, claiming it always belonged.
g: The point laundered money looks clean, which is exactly why catching it earlier matters.

p: Deliberately breaking a large sum into smaller transactions to slip under reporting thresholds like the [[CTR|CTR]] trigger, a tactic also known as [[Smurfing|smurfing]] that [[AML|AML]] controls are built to catch.
x: Chopping a suspicious lump of cash into bite-size pieces so no single one rings the alarm.
g: Nothing legitimate, but it's a classic red flag that monitoring is built to catch.

p: A form of [[Structuring|structuring]] using many people or accounts to make lots of small deposits that each stay under the radar.
x: Recruiting an army of little blue helpers to each carry a harmless-looking handful of cash.
g: Nothing good, but it's a textbook laundering pattern detection models hunt for.

p: Disguising illicit value through mis-invoiced, over- or under-valued, or entirely phantom trade shipments, one of the largest and hardest-to-spot channels that [[AML]] regimes try to catch.
x: Laundering money by pretending a container of socks is worth a million pounds, or pretending it exists at all.
g: Nothing legitimate, but it's one of the largest and hardest-to-spot laundering channels.

p: Heavier [[CDD|due diligence]] applied to higher-risk customers, like [[PEP|PEPs]] or those in risky jurisdictions, digging into [[Source of Funds|source of funds]] and [[Source of Wealth|source of wealth]].
x: The full background check you get when something about you makes compliance sit up a little straighter.
g: Letting firms bank higher-risk customers without flying blind.

p: The origin of the specific money used in a transaction, as opposed to the customer's overall wealth.
x: The 'where did this particular pile come from?' question, distinct from how you got rich generally.
g: Pinning down whether the money in a given deal is clean.

p: The origin of a customer's total accumulated assets, a core part of [[EDD|Enhanced Due Diligence]] for higher-risk clients.
x: The 'how did you get rich in the first place?' question, asked politely but very seriously.
g: Understanding whether someone's overall wealth has a legitimate story.

p: Checking customers against negative news and media for signs of financial crime, fraud or other risk, a complement to [[Sanctions Screening|sanctions screening]] and the [[Watchlist|watchlist]] checks that only catch the already-listed.
x: Googling your customer, but industrial-grade, to see if they've made the papers for the wrong reasons.
g: Catching risk that lists and documents miss but the press has already reported.

p: Continuously refreshing customer [[KYC|due diligence]] as things change, rather than dusting it off every few years.
x: Swapping the occasional awkward 'please reconfirm everything' for quietly watching all the time.
g: Keeping risk profiles current without periodic mass re-verification scrambles.

p: A consolidated list of sanctioned, politically exposed or otherwise high-risk entities that payments and customers are screened against during [[Sanctions Screening|sanctions screening]], with the [[PEP]] entries flagging the politically exposed.
x: The naughty list compliance checks every name against, hoping for no surprises.
g: Stopping payments to, or onboarding of, people firms are legally barred from serving.

p: A screening or monitoring alert that turns out to be harmless, the bane of any [[Transaction Monitoring|monitoring]] team's day.
x: The fire alarm going off because someone made toast, times ten thousand, every day.
g: Nothing in itself, but cutting false positives is the holy grail of efficient compliance.

p: The report a firm files when activity looks like financial crime, the international cousin of the [[SAR]] and a counterpart to the cash-triggered [[CTR]].
x: Compliance's formal 'this smells off' note, posted to the authorities just in case.
g: Flagging dodgy activity to regulators while staying the right side of the law.

p: A mandatory US report for cash transactions above 10,000 dollars, the threshold that [[Structuring|structuring]] tries to dodge, and a sibling of the suspicion-triggered [[STR]].
x: The paperwork that automatically fires when you move a suspiciously round wad of cash.
g: Giving authorities visibility of large cash movements.

p: The named individual personally responsible for a firm's anti-money-laundering controls and its [[SAR]] submissions.
x: The one person whose neck is on the line if the laundering checks go wrong.
g: Putting a single accountable human behind a firm's financial-crime defences.

p: Real-time checking of individual payments against sanctions and [[Watchlist|watchlists]] before they're allowed to settle, a form of [[Sanctions Screening|sanctions screening]] distinct from ongoing [[Transaction Monitoring|transaction monitoring]].
x: The split-second name check on every payment, asking 'are we legally allowed to send this?'.
g: Blocking sanctioned or prohibited payments before the money leaves.

p: An informal, trust-based value-transfer system that moves money across borders without the funds physically travelling, settling between brokers later.
x: Sending money abroad on a handshake and a phone call, no bank, no wire, just trust and a ledger.
g: Moving value cheaply where banking is thin, though its opacity worries regulators.

p: Fraud using a fabricated identity stitched together from a mix of real and made-up details, often nurtured for months before the bust-out.
x: A Frankenstein person built from a real number here, a fake name there, then handed a credit card.
g: Nothing good, but it's the fastest-growing fraud type, so naming it matters.

p: Submitting false information on a credit or account application to obtain funds or access fraudulently.
x: Lying on the form to get the card, loan or account you'd never legitimately be given.
g: Nothing legitimate, but it's the front door most fraud walks through.

p: Fraud committed using a real victim's identity without their knowledge, the counterpart to [[First-Party Fraud|first-party fraud]].
x: Someone else wearing your identity like a stolen coat, while you get the bill.
g: Nothing good, but distinguishing it from first-party fraud shapes how it's fought.

p: When a genuine customer disputes a purchase they actually made, the everyday label for much [[Chargeback Fraud|chargeback fraud]] and [[First-Party Fraud|first-party fraud]].
x: 'I never bought this', says the person who definitely bought this, to claw back the cash.
g: Nothing legitimate, but it's the single biggest dispute headache merchants face.

p: A long-con scam blending fake romance with a bogus investment, grooming victims for weeks before draining them, often via crypto, where the [[Romance Scam|romance scam]] is the hook and the [[Investment Scam|investment scam]] is the kill.
x: A patient, cruel scam that fattens the victim with affection and fake gains before the slaughter.
g: Nothing good, but it's the defining scam of the era, so awareness is the defence.

p: Fraud exploiting a fake romantic relationship to extract money, a common form of [[APP Scam]] and the emotional hook at the heart of [[Pig Butchering|pig butchering]].
x: Falling for someone who only ever existed to fall for your bank balance.
g: Nothing legitimate, but naming the pattern helps people and banks spot it early.

p: Demanding an upfront payment for a promised larger payout, prize or loan that never arrives, one of the oldest forms of [[APP Scam]].
x: The classic 'pay a small fee to unlock your millions' con, prince optional.
g: Nothing good, but it's the granddaddy of scams, endlessly reinvented.

p: Re-targeting previous fraud victims by posing as a fund-recovery service that, for a fee, supposedly gets their money back, a parasitic follow-up to an earlier [[APP Scam|APP scam]].
x: Robbing the already-robbed by promising to undo the first robbery.
g: Nothing good, but warning victims of the second hit is the whole point of naming it.

p: Brute-forcing valid card numbers by churning through combinations within a known [[BIN]] range, a cousin of [[Card Testing|card testing]].
x: A robot guessing card numbers like a safecracker spinning the dial, until something clicks.
g: Nothing legitimate, but spotting the pattern lets issuers shut it down fast.

p: A three-way scam where a fake storefront takes real orders, then fulfils them using stolen cards, pocketing the clean money.
x: A bogus shop in the middle, taking your money and someone else's card to ship you a real toaster.
g: Nothing good, but the three-cornered shape is exactly what makes it detectable.

p: Exploiting [[Refund|refund]] and returns policies to extract money or goods you're not entitled to, sometimes as a paid 'service'.
x: Gaming the returns desk for cash or freebies, occasionally with professional help.
g: Nothing legitimate, but it's a fast-rising abuse merchants increasingly screen for.

p: Patiently building good credit on an account, then maxing every available line at once with no intention to repay.
x: Being the model customer right up until the day you vanish with everything the bank would lend.
g: Nothing good, but recognising the slow build is how lenders catch it before the bust.

p: An account that gathers many small deposits from multiple people or places, often fed by a [[Money Mule|money mule]] and the deposit-splitting trick of [[Structuring|structuring]], then rapidly moves the pooled funds out.
x: A drain that collects dribbles of suspicious cash from everywhere, then flushes it all at once.
g: Nothing legitimate, but the funnel shape is a textbook laundering signal.

p: Fraud detection that analyses how you type, swipe and hold your device, rather than what you know or own.
x: Your bank recognising you by the rhythm of your thumbs, like a digital walk you can't fake.
g: Spotting impostors silently, without making real users jump through hoops.

p: Checking that a biometric sample comes from a real, present person rather than a photo, video or deepfake.
x: The 'blink and turn your head' moment that stops fraudsters holding up a printout of your face.
g: Stopping spoofed selfies and deepfakes at identity checks.

p: [[Social Engineering]] via email (phishing), SMS (smishing) and voice calls (vishing) to harvest credentials or trigger payments.
x: The same con in three outfits: a dodgy email, a dodgy text and a dodgy phone call.
g: Nothing good, but naming the channels is how people learn to distrust them.

p: Malware that hands a fraudster remote control of a victim's device, letting them authorise payments as if they were the user.
x: A burglar who doesn't break in but quietly takes the wheel of your computer from afar.
g: Nothing legitimate, but it's a key enabler of [[Account Takeover|account takeover]] worth understanding.

p: Human examination of a flagged transaction or application before it's approved or declined.
x: When the robots get nervous and call in a person to squint at your order.
g: Catching the nuanced fraud (and sparing the false alarms) that rules and models miss.

p: A deterministic if-this-then-that system that applies fixed fraud and risk rules to transactions, often alongside machine-learning models.
x: The fraud system's rulebook half: blunt, explainable and occasionally too literal for its own good.
g: Encoding clear-cut risk policies that need no training data and can be explained to a regulator.

p: An [[SCA Exemption]] letting low-risk payments skip [[SCA|strong authentication]] based on the provider's own fraud rates.
x: Earning the right to wave safe-looking payments through, as long as your fraud stays low enough.
g: Reducing checkout friction without breaching the rules, for low-risk transactions.

p: An [[SCA Exemption]] where a cardholder marks a merchant as trusted, so future payments to it can skip [[SCA|authentication]].
x: Adding a shop to your 'these are fine' list so it stops asking you to prove yourself every time.
g: Smoothing repeat purchases at merchants you already trust.

p: When an issuer lets a wallet, merchant or other party perform [[SCA|strong customer authentication]] on its behalf.
x: The bank outsourcing the 'is it really you?' check to someone it trusts to ask on its behalf.
g: Letting a smooth wallet or merchant flow satisfy authentication rules for the bank.

p: The set of systems that touch card data and therefore fall under [[PCI DSS]] obligations, which everyone tries to shrink.
x: The blast radius of your card handling: the smaller it is, the less of your estate the auditors crawl over.
g: Focusing security effort, and cutting compliance cost, by minimising what touches card data.

p: The [[PCI DSS]] checklist a merchant completes to validate compliance, with a version tailored to how they handle card data.
x: The 'tick to confirm you're being careful with cards' form, in nine flavours of tedium.
g: Letting smaller merchants prove PCI compliance without a full external audit.

p: A [[PCI DSS|PCI]]-certified assessor who runs on-site audits and signs off a merchant's Report on Compliance.
x: The professional who shows up to check you really are guarding card data, not just claiming to.
g: Independently validating PCI compliance for larger, higher-risk merchants.

p: The US rule implementing the Electronic Fund Transfer Act, setting consumers' rights and liability limits for electronic payments. Its credit-side sibling is [[Regulation Z]].
x: The American rulebook deciding who eats the loss when an electronic payment goes wrong.
g: Protecting US consumers when electronic transfers are unauthorised or erroneous.

p: The US Truth in Lending rule governing credit disclosures and the [[Chargeback|dispute]] rights behind credit-card protections. Its electronic-payments sibling is [[Regulation E]].
x: The fine-print law that makes US lenders actually tell you what your credit costs.
g: Giving US credit-card holders disclosure and dispute rights.

p: A US regulatory category covering money transmitters, currency exchangers and similar firms, registered with FinCEN.
x: The official US label for 'a business that moves or swaps money for people', with all the rules that brings.
g: Bringing money movers into the [[AML]] and licensing net.

p: The state-by-state US licence required to move money on others' behalf, famously needed in nearly every state separately.
x: The reason launching US payments means collecting roughly fifty licences like very expensive stamps.
g: Legally authorising a firm to transmit money across the US, one state at a time.

p: The [[FCA]]'s outcomes-based regime requiring firms to deliver good outcomes and fair value for retail customers.
x: The UK regulator telling firms 'don't just avoid breaking rules, actually be good to customers'.
g: Raising the bar from 'technically compliant' to 'genuinely fair' in UK financial services.

p: The EU rule capping card [[Interchange|interchange]] at 0.2 percent for debit and 0.3 percent for credit, the European cousin of the [[Durbin Amendment]].
x: Brussels deciding card fees had got silly and simply capping them by law.
g: Cutting the card fees merchants pay across the EU.

p: The new EU anti-money-laundering supervisor, based in Frankfurt, created under the 2024 EU [[AML]] package.
x: Europe's freshly minted laundering watchdog, built to stop dirty money slipping between national cracks.
g: Bringing consistent, central AML supervision across the EU.

p: The directly-applicable 'single rulebook' harmonising anti-money-laundering obligations across the EU, centrepiece of the 2024 package.
x: One AML rulebook to bind them all, so firms stop juggling 27 national variations.
g: Standardising [[AML]] rules across every EU member state at once.

p: The directive strand of the EU's anti-money-laundering reforms, toughening offences and liability.
x: The sixth sequel in Brussels' long-running war on laundering, this time with sharper teeth.
g: Hardening criminal liability for money laundering across the EU.

p: The bank-owned US network that moves money between American bank accounts in minutes, built straight into banking apps.
x: America's answer to just transferring money, bolted onto your banking app so you don't need a separate one.
g: Sending money between US bank accounts fast, without a third-party wallet.

p: PayPal's US peer-to-peer wallet, known for its social feed of who paid whom (if not always why).
x: The app that turned splitting a dinner bill into a public performance, emoji included.
g: Splitting costs and paying friends in the US, sociably.

p: Block's consumer payments app for sending money, holding balances and dabbling in stocks and bitcoin, big in the US.
x: Part wallet, part brokerage, part 'why do I have a $7 balance in here', beloved stateside.
g: Moving money, and more, for US consumers from one app.

p: Canada's near-ubiquitous bank transfer, addressed by email or phone number and often unlocked with a security question.
x: How Canadians pay each other: an email, a secret question, and money politely appears.
g: Sending money between Canadian accounts using just an email or mobile number.

p: Poland's mobile payment scheme using one-time six-digit codes generated in your banking app for instant bank payments online, in-store and at ATMs. A member of the [[EuroPA]] alliance.
x: The Polish six-digit code you punch in to pay, no card, no fuss, just a number with a short shelf life.
g: Paying instantly from a Polish bank account anywhere, with a throwaway code.

p: Sweden's phone-number instant payment app, jointly built by the banks and used by most of the population.
x: So woven into Swedish life that 'to Swish someone' is just a verb now.
g: Paying people and merchants instantly in Sweden via a mobile number.

p: The merged Norwegian, Danish and Finnish mobile wallet for peer-to-peer and retail payments across the Nordics. A member of the [[EuroPA]] alliance.
x: What you get when the Nordics decide three national payment apps should just be one.
g: Paying friends and shops across Norway, Denmark and Finland from one app.

p: Switzerland's mobile payment app, linking bank accounts to pay by QR or Bluetooth in shops and between people.
x: The Swiss app that quietly handles everything from market stalls to splitting fondue.
g: Paying instantly from a Swiss bank account, in person or peer-to-peer.

p: Spain's bank-backed instant payment service, sending money between people via their phone numbers. A member of the [[EuroPA]] alliance.
x: The Spanish 'just Bizum me' that replaced cash for splitting tapas.
g: Sending instant payments between Spanish bank accounts using a mobile number.

p: Portugal's mobile instant-payment and card-[[Tokenisation|tokenisation]] app, run by SIBS, operator of the national Multibanco network. A member of the [[EuroPA]] alliance.
x: Portugal's do-it-all payment app, from splitting bills to spinning up virtual cards.
g: Paying, transferring and tokenising cards instantly in Portugal.

p: Mexico's central-bank-run interbank system for near-instant electronic transfers between accounts.
x: The Mexican rail doing the real work behind the scenes whenever money zips between banks.
g: Settling fast interbank transfers across Mexico.

p: The pioneering East African [[Mobile Money|mobile money]] service that lets people store and send value over basic phones, no bank account required.
x: The phone-credit-turned-currency that banked millions who'd never seen a branch.
g: Bringing payments and savings to people far from any bank, via a humble handset.

p: Malaysia's instant payment and QR rail, letting people pay to a proxy like a phone number or national ID.
x: Malaysia's 'pay to my phone number' system, with a QR code for good measure.
g: Paying instantly across Malaysia without swapping account numbers.

p: Australia's overlay service for instant payments, run by BPAY on top of the [[NPP]] rails.
x: The friendly badge on an Australian payment that means 'this lands in seconds, not days'.
g: Delivering near-instant bank payments in Australia.

p: India's 24/7 instant interbank transfer service, the round-the-clock rail that predated [[UPI]] and the faster sibling of batch-based [[NEFT]].
x: India's original always-on money mover, still humming under plenty of payments today.
g: Sending instant interbank payments in India at any hour.

p: India's deferred, batch-settled retail transfer system, the slower-but-steady sibling of [[IMPS]] and [[UPI]].
x: The patient Indian rail that gathers payments into batches rather than racing each one through.
g: Moving retail payments across India when instant isn't essential.

exp: Nigeria Inter-Bank Settlement System Instant Payments
p: Nigeria's backbone instant interbank transfer system, run by the Nigeria Inter-Bank Settlement System.
x: The rail quietly powering the 'I've sent it, check your account' moment across Nigeria.
g: Settling instant bank transfers nationwide in Nigeria.

p: Peru's two dominant bank-backed peer-to-peer wallets, used to send money instantly by phone number or QR.
x: How Peruvians pay each other now, to the point the apps' names are basically verbs.
g: Sending instant everyday payments between people in Peru.

p: Colombia's central-bank instant payment system, launched to interconnect wallets and banks for low-cost transfers.
x: Colombia's freshly built rail for making 'send me the money' actually instant and cheap.
g: Connecting Colombian banks and wallets for instant, low-cost payments.

p: Kenya's interbank instant rail, linking banks (and wallets) for direct account-to-account transfers.
x: The Kenyan rail letting banks talk to each other directly, no detour required.
g: Moving money instantly between Kenyan bank accounts.

p: The Philippines' dominant e-wallets, used for payments, transfers, bills and increasingly banking.
x: The two apps most Filipino phones can't do without, for paying just about anything.
g: Handling everyday digital payments for millions in the Philippines.

p: The Philippines' two interbank rails: InstaPay for real-time low-value transfers, PESONet for same-day batch payments.
x: The Filipino tag-team, one sprinter for instant transfers, one steady hauler for batches.
g: Covering both instant and bulk interbank payments across the Philippines.

p: Extensions of India's [[UPI]]: Lite for low-value on-device payments, AutoPay for recurring e-mandates, and 123Pay for feature phones without internet.
x: UPI's family of spin-offs, covering tiny payments, subscriptions and even phones that can barely browse.
g: Stretching UPI to small change, [[Recurring Billing|recurring billing]] and the unconnected.

p: India's home-grown card network, now linkable to [[UPI]], offering a domestic alternative to the global schemes.
x: India's own card brand, built so the country's payments don't all run through foreign rails.
g: Keeping Indian card payments domestic, cheap and increasingly UPI-connected.

p: The North American standard for open-finance data sharing, the market-led counterpart to Europe's regulator-driven [[Open Banking]].
x: America building its open-banking plumbing by industry agreement rather than government decree.
g: Standardising how US and Canadian banks share data with apps.

p: An [[Open Banking]] authentication where you approve in your bank's app on a separate device, with no redirect from the merchant's page.
x: Paying on your laptop while the actual yes happens, untethered, in your phone's banking app.
g: Authenticating cleanly when a redirect or pop-up would break the experience. The card and [[3-D Secure|3DS]] analogue is [[Decoupled Authentication|decoupled authentication]].

p: An [[Open Banking]] authentication where you enter your bank credentials inside the third party's own interface rather than the bank's.
x: Typing your bank login into someone else's screen, which is exactly why regulators eye it warily.
g: Keeping users in one interface, where the bank permits it. Not to be confused with [[Embedded Checkout|embedded checkout]], a merchant-side checkout interface rather than an authentication model. Compare the card world's [[HPP|Hosted Payment Page]], where the sensitive step is instead hosted away on the provider's page.

p: A regulated digital certificate proving the identity of an open-banking provider to a bank, part of the EU's eIDAS trust framework.
x: The cryptographic ID badge a fintech flashes so your bank knows it's a real, authorised party.
g: Letting banks trust that an open-banking request comes from a legitimate provider.

p: A regulated certificate used to sign open-banking data so its origin and integrity can be verified, the sealing counterpart to a [[QWAC]].
x: The wax seal of open banking, proving a message really came from who it claims and wasn't tampered with.
g: Guaranteeing the authenticity and integrity of shared open-banking data.

p: The [[Open Banking]] rule that required customers to re-confirm consent for ongoing data access every 90 days, long criticised for breaking connections.
x: The recurring 'please log in again' that quietly severed many an open-banking link every three months.
g: Periodically reconfirming consent, though newer rules are relaxing the friction.

p: Pulling multiple bank accounts into a single view via [[AIS|account information services]], so all your balances live in one place.
x: Herding all your scattered accounts into one screen, so you can despair at the total in comfort.
g: Giving people and apps one consolidated picture across many banks.

p: Paying to a simple proxy like a phone number, email or ID instead of a full account number, with the rail resolving it behind the scenes. Also called proxy addressing.
x: Sending money to a phone number and trusting the system to find the actual account, no IBAN gymnastics.
g: Making account-to-account payments as easy as knowing someone's mobile number.

p: The umbrella term in [[SEPA]] for payments that don't go cleanly: Rejects, Returns, Refunds, Reversals and Recalls.
x: The catch-all for every way a euro payment can come boomeranging back at you.
g: Naming the family of exceptions so banks can handle bounced and clawed-back payments.

p: The bank screen where customers can see, manage and revoke the [[Open Banking|open-banking]] access they've granted to third parties.
x: The 'who did I give permission to, and why are some of these still here?' control panel.
g: Letting people review and cut off data access they no longer want.

p: Bank APIs that go beyond the regulated [[Open Banking]] minimum, offered commercially for richer data or payment features.
x: The paid tier of open banking, where banks sell the good stuff the rules don't force them to give away.
g: Unlocking enhanced data and features beyond the regulatory baseline. In the EU, formalised by the [[SPAA]] scheme.

p: The two bank roles in a US [[ACH]] transfer: the originating bank that sends the entry and the receiving bank that collects it.
x: The sender's bank and the receiver's bank, given suitably gladiatorial acronyms.
g: Pinning down which institution does what in every ACH payment.

p: A zero-amount [[ACH]] entry sent to validate account details before real payments start flowing.
x: A polite test knock on an account to check it exists before you trust it with actual money.
g: Catching wrong account details before a live ACH payment fails.

p: The three-letter code on a US [[ACH]] entry describing the type of payment, such as WEB, PPD or CCD.
x: The little ACH label saying whether a payment came from the web, a payroll run or a business deal.
g: Telling the ACH network how a payment was authorised and what rules apply.

p: An [[ACH]] message from a receiving bank telling the originator to correct outdated account details for future payments.
x: The bank's gentle 'these details have moved on, please update your records' nudge.
g: Keeping recurring ACH payment details accurate over time.

p: A reference that ties a specific [[SEPA]] [[Direct Debit|direct debit]] to its underlying authorisation, so each collection can be traced to a [[Mandate|mandate]], and pairs with the [[SEPA Creditor Identifier]] that names the biller.
x: The serial number stapled to a direct-debit permission, so everyone knows which agreement a charge belongs to.
g: Linking every direct-debit collection back to the mandate that authorised it.

p: The unique ID assigned to an organisation that collects [[SEPA]] [[Direct Debit|direct debits]], identifying the biller across all its mandates while the [[Unique Mandate Reference|unique mandate reference]] pins down each one, under either [[SEPA Direct Debit Core & B2B|Core or B2B]] rules.
x: The collector's official 'this is who's pulling the money' badge in the SEPA system.
g: Identifying who is behind every SEPA direct-debit collection.

p: The two [[SEPA]] [[Direct Debit|direct debit]] schemes: Core for consumers, with refund rights, and B2B for businesses, faster but with no refund right. Each biller is named by a [[SEPA Creditor Identifier]] and each collection traced through its [[Unique Mandate Reference|unique mandate reference]].
x: Two flavours of euro direct debit: the consumer one with a safety net, the business one without.
g: Matching the right direct-debit rules to consumer versus corporate payers.

p: A payment where only one side, the payer or the payee, sits inside a given scheme or currency zone, the other being beyond it.
x: A payment with one foot inside the club and one foot out in the cold.
g: Handling [[Cross-Border Payments|cross-border payments]] where only one end is in the local scheme.

p: The European Central Bank's service for settling euro instant payments in central-bank money, around the clock, the rail beneath much of [[SEPA Instant]].
x: The Eurosystem's always-on engine making sure instant euro payments actually settle, not just promise to.
g: Settling euro instant payments safely in central-bank money, 24/7.

p: EBA Clearing's pan-European instant payment system, settling [[SEPA Instant]] credit transfers across the continent.
x: The privately-run sibling to the ECB's instant rail, also racing euros across Europe in seconds.
g: Clearing instant euro payments at pan-European scale.

p: The Eurosystem's [[RTGS|real-time gross settlement]] system for high-value euro payments between banks, now modernised as T2.
x: The big-ticket euro plumbing where banks settle the serious money, one large payment at a time.
g: Settling large-value euro payments with finality between European banks.

p: The US Federal Reserve's [[RTGS|real-time gross settlement]] system for high-value dollar [[Wire Transfer|wires]].
x: America's heavyweight dollar rail, where the big interbank money moves with central-bank finality.
g: Settling large, time-critical dollar payments in the US with finality.

p: A privately-run US system that nets large-value dollar payments before settling, complementing [[Fedwire]].
x: Fedwire's netting-minded cousin, batching up big dollar payments so less cash has to actually move.
g: Settling high-value dollar payments efficiently by netting them down first.

p: Settling the [[Netting|netted]] positions of many payments at set times, rather than one by one in real time as [[RTGS]] does, the delayed form of [[Net Settlement|net settlement]].
x: The 'let's tally everyone up and settle the difference later' approach, easier on liquidity than paying as you go.
g: Saving liquidity by settling net balances at intervals instead of payment by payment.

p: The classic legacy [[SWIFT]] message for a single customer [[Credit Transfer|credit transfer]], the workhorse format behind countless cross-border [[Wire Transfer|wires]].
x: The grizzled veteran message format that's moved the world's money for decades, now being gently retired.
g: Instructing a cross-border customer payment between banks.

p: The [[ISO 20022]] message for a bank-to-bank customer [[Credit Transfer|credit transfer]], the modern successor to the [[MT103]].
x: MT103's data-rich replacement, carrying far more detail about who's paying whom and why.
g: Moving a customer payment between banks with structured, modern data.

p: The [[ISO 20022]] message a customer sends their bank to initiate a payment, the 'please make this payment' instruction.
x: The 'pain' you send your bank to kick off a payment, aptly named for anyone who's debugged one.
g: Letting a business instruct its bank to make payments in a standard format.

p: The [[ISO 20022]] end-of-day bank statement message, reporting account activity back for [[Reconciliation|reconciliation]].
x: Your bank's structured 'here's everything that happened today' statement, built for machines to read.
g: Feeding automated reconciliation with clean, structured statement data.

p: The unique ID stamped on a payment so it can be tracked across banks, the reference that powers [[SWIFT gpi]] tracking.
x: The parcel-tracking number for a payment, so you can finally see where your money is stuck.
g: Tracing a payment end to end across multiple banks.

p: A coded reason-for-payment field carried in [[ISO 20022]] and many local rails, declaring what a payment is actually for.
x: The little dropdown that makes a payment explain itself: salary, invoice, rent, or 'other'.
g: Telling banks and regulators why money is moving, for compliance and routing.

p: The industry-wide shift from older formats like [[ISO 8583]] and [[SWIFT]] MT to the richer [[ISO 20022]] standard.
x: The great payments house-move from cramped old message formats to a roomier, data-rich new one.
g: Modernising payment messaging so richer data flows end to end.

p: The daily deadline after which a payment is processed on the next [[Value Date|value date]] or cycle rather than today.
x: The payments equivalent of the last post: miss it and your money waits until tomorrow.
g: Setting clear deadlines so banks know which day a payment belongs to.

p: Making sure the right funds sit in the right accounts and currencies to meet [[Settlement|settlement]] obligations as they fall due.
x: The treasury art of never being caught short, having enough money in exactly the right pocket at the right moment.
g: Keeping a firm solvent in practice, with cash where and when it's needed.

p: Virtually offsetting the balances of many accounts for interest and overdraft purposes, without physically moving any money.
x: Letting your accounts mentally hold hands for interest, even though the cash never actually mingles.
g: Optimising interest across many accounts without shuffling funds around.

p: Physically sweeping balances from many accounts into one master account, the moving-money counterpart to [[Notional Pooling|notional pooling]].
x: Hoovering scattered balances into a single pot so the money's all in one place to work.
g: Centralising a group's cash so it can be managed and invested as one.

p: A temporary holding account for payments that can't yet be matched or allocated, pending investigation.
x: The lost-property cupboard of finance, where unidentified money waits to be claimed.
g: Parking unmatched funds safely until [[Reconciliation|reconciliation]] sorts them out.

p: A mismatch between two records that [[Reconciliation|reconciliation]] has to investigate and resolve, the gremlin in the books.
x: The 'these two numbers should agree but don't' moment that ruins an accountant's afternoon.
g: Flagging exactly where records disagree so the error can be hunted down.

p: The processes and queues for payments that fall out of [[STP|Straight-Through Processing]] and need human intervention, akin to the [[Repair Queue|repair queue]].
x: The triage ward for payments that didn't go through cleanly and now need a human to look at them.
g: Catching and fixing the payments that automation couldn't process on its own.

p: A corporate-treasury operating model that routes all of a company group's outbound payments through one central function with standardised processes and controls, often paying on behalf of subsidiaries. Not to be confused with a [[Payment Hub|payment hub]], the technology platform it often runs on.
x: One central payments department for the whole company, instead of every subsidiary doing its own thing.
g: Standardising and centralising a corporate group's own payments, cutting cost and risk.

p: A treasury structure where one central entity provides banking services, like loans, [[FX]] and pooling, to a group's own subsidiaries.
x: A company running its own internal bank, so subsidiaries borrow and lend to each other instead of outsiders.
g: Letting a group fund and net itself internally, cutting external banking costs.

p: Two linked ideas behind resilient payments. Redundancy is having spare, duplicated systems, processors or rails standing ready; failover is the act of automatically switching over to them the moment the primary one fails, so payments keep flowing.
x: Redundancy is keeping a spare tyre in the boot; failover is the car swapping onto it mid-motorway without you lifting a finger.
g: Keeping payments running through outages, a backbone of [[Operational Resilience|operational resilience]].

p: Representing off-chain assets like Treasuries, money-market funds, gold or property as transferable tokens on a [[Blockchain|blockchain]], each carrying a legal claim.
x: Putting boring real-world assets on-chain so they can be traded at internet speed instead of bank speed.
g: Letting traditional assets settle and move with crypto's speed and programmability.

p: Making crypto wallets behave like programmable smart accounts, with features like recovery, batching and sponsored fees, rather than raw key-pairs.
x: Finally letting crypto wallets work like normal apps, so losing your keys isn't instantly catastrophic.
g: Bringing consumer-grade usability and safety nets to self-custody wallets.

p: A smart-contract service that sponsors or lets users pay [[Gas Fees|network fees]] in a token like [[USDC]] instead of the chain's native coin.
x: The thing that means you don't need a stash of obscure crypto just to send the crypto you actually have.
g: Making on-chain payments feel gasless and friendly to normal users.

p: Securing crypto by splitting a [[Private Key|private key]] into shares computed jointly, so no single person or machine ever holds the whole key.
x: Chopping the master key into pieces and scattering them, so no one thief can ever steal the lot.
g: Protecting crypto holdings without a single catastrophic point of failure.

p: Cryptographic or auditor-backed evidence that an issuer genuinely holds the assets backing every token in circulation, the reassurance that a [[Depeg|depeg]] hasn't quietly hollowed out the backing.
x: A [[Stablecoin|stablecoin]] issuer showing its receipts, so you don't have to just trust that the dollars are really there.
g: Reassuring holders that a stablecoin or exchange is actually backed, not bluffing.

p: When a [[Stablecoin|stablecoin]] breaks from its target value, slipping above or (more alarmingly) below its peg, the very failure that [[Proof of Reserves|proof of reserves]] is meant to guard against.
x: The heart-stopping moment a 'one dollar' coin starts trading at ninety-six cents.
g: Nothing good, but it's the key failure mode anyone holding stablecoins must understand.

p: Creating new tokens (mint) on deposit and destroying them (burn) on redemption, the mechanism that keeps a [[Stablecoin|stablecoin]] supply matched to its reserves.
x: The financial equivalent of printing a token when money comes in and shredding it when money goes out.
g: Keeping a token's supply honestly tied to the assets behind it.

p: Infrastructure that moves tokens or value between separate blockchains, locking on one side and issuing on the other.
x: The rickety-but-essential crossing between blockchains, and, notoriously, a favourite target for hackers.
g: Letting value flow between otherwise-isolated chains.

p: A token representing an asset from another chain one-to-one, like wrapped bitcoin on Ethereum, so it can be used where the original can't go.
x: An asset in fancy dress, wearing another chain's costume so it can join that chain's party.
g: Using an asset's value on a [[Blockchain|blockchain]] it doesn't natively live on.

p: The global FATF term for a regulated crypto business such as an exchange or custodian, bound by anti-money-laundering rules including the [[Travel Rule]], known in the EU under [[MiCA]] as a [[CASP]].
x: The official label that turns 'a crypto company' into 'a regulated entity with compliance obligations'.
g: Bringing crypto firms into the same financial-crime rules as banks.

p: The [[MiCA]] term for an authorised EU crypto firm, the European counterpart to a [[VASP]].
x: What a [[VASP]] is called once it has to answer to Brussels.
g: Identifying the licensed crypto businesses regulated under MiCA.

p: A [[MiCA]] [[Crypto-asset|crypto-asset]] pegged to a single fiat currency and treated much like [[E-money|e-money]], the EU's category for fiat [[Stablecoin|stablecoins]], distinct from the multi-asset-backed [[Asset-Referenced Token|asset-referenced token]].
x: Europe's tidy legal box for 'a token that's basically a digital euro or dollar'.
g: Giving fiat-backed stablecoins a clear regulated status in the EU.

p: A [[MiCA]] [[Crypto-asset|crypto-asset]] referencing a basket of currencies, commodities or assets, rather than a single currency like an [[E-Money Token|e-money token]].
x: MiCA's box for the more exotic stablecoins that track a mix of things instead of just one dollar.
g: Regulating multi-asset-backed tokens distinctly from single-currency ones.

p: Holding your own [[Private Key|keys]] with no intermediary in control, the principle behind a [[Non-Custodial Wallet|non-custodial wallet]].
x: Being your own bank, with all the freedom and all the terrifying responsibility that implies.
g: Giving people full control of their crypto, answerable to no custodian.

p: A human-readable list of words that backs up and restores a crypto wallet's [[Private Key|keys]].
x: The twelve magic words that ARE your money, which is why writing them on a sticky note is a horror story.
g: Letting you recover [[Self-Custody|self-custodied]] crypto if a device is lost.

p: The secret cryptographic value that authorises spending from a crypto address, the thing that truly equals ownership.
x: The one secret that, if someone else learns it, means the money was never really yours.
g: Proving ownership and authorising every move of on-chain funds.

p: On-chain shares of a money-market fund that can settle near-instantly and serve as collateral, a flagship case of [[RWA Tokenisation]].
x: A boring, yield-paying fund reborn as a blockchain token you can move in seconds.
g: Earning yield on idle cash while keeping it instantly usable on-chain.

p: A Mexican cash-voucher method where shoppers pay for online orders in cash at OXXO convenience stores.
x: How a huge slice of Mexico buys online: order on the web, then pay the cashier in actual notes.
g: Letting people without cards or banks pay for online purchases in cash.

p: A Brazilian printable or scannable voucher, paid in cash or via online banking, widely used for online and offline purchases.
x: Brazil's beloved payment slip: a barcode you settle at a bank, app or shop, no card required.
g: Reaching Brazil's many cash-preferring and unbanked shoppers.

p: A Japanese method where customers pay for online orders in cash at convenience stores, the local cousin of [[OXXO]].
x: Paying for your internet shopping at the 7-Eleven counter, a very Japanese kind of convenient.
g: Serving Japan's persistent appetite for paying in cash.

p: Portugal's interbank network whose reference-number method lets shoppers pay online orders via ATM or online banking.
x: The Portuguese 'here's a reference, go pay it at any cashpoint' way of buying online.
g: Letting Portuguese shoppers pay without sharing card details.

p: Charging a digital purchase straight to the customer's mobile phone bill instead of a card or bank account.
x: Buying that game add-on and quietly shunting the cost onto next month's phone bill.
g: Letting people buy digital goods with no card, just a phone number.

p: A prepaid balance held against a card or account, like a [[Gift Card|gift card]] or wallet top-up, rather than a linked bank or credit line.
x: Money you've parked in advance inside a card or app, waiting to be spent.
g: Pre-loading funds for gift cards, transit, wallets and closed systems.

p: The standard [[BNPL]] product splitting a purchase into four interest-free instalments, usually a fortnight apart, as opposed to the lump-sum [[Pay in 30]].
x: The 'why pay all at once?' button that turns one purchase into four future flinches.
g: Spreading a cost over a few weeks at no interest, lifting checkout conversion.

p: A [[BNPL]] variant that defers the whole payment by around 30 days, with no instalments, popularised for try-before-you-buy, unlike the four-instalment [[Pay in 4]].
x: Buy now, panic in a month, the deferred-payment option that lets you send the wrong size back first.
g: Letting shoppers try goods before paying, cutting hesitation and returns friction.

p: Extra line-item and tax detail submitted on business and government card payments to unlock cheaper [[Interchange|interchange]].
x: The extra paperwork on a corporate card payment that quietly earns the merchant a discount.
g: Cutting card costs on B2B and public-sector transactions through richer data.

p: A pricing model that exposes [[Interchange|interchange]], [[Scheme Fees|scheme fees]] and the processor's markup as three separate, visible components, going further than [[Interchange-Plus|interchange-plus]].
x: The most see-through card pricing there is: every fee laid out so you know exactly who took what.
g: Giving merchants full transparency into what each part of a card fee costs.

p: The card network's own slice taken on every transaction, separate from [[Interchange|interchange]], which goes to the issuer, and one component of the broader [[Scheme Fees|scheme fees]].
x: The scheme's cut for running the network, quietly riding along on top of interchange.
g: Funding the card networks themselves, distinct from what issuers earn.

p: Total card fees divided by total card volume, the real all-in percentage a merchant actually pays to accept cards, much like the [[MDR]] but measured after the fact and a sharper way to judge an [[Interchange-Plus|interchange-plus]] deal.
x: The single honest number behind all the pricing spin: what taking cards genuinely costs you.
g: Letting merchants benchmark and compare their true cost of accepting cards.

p: When a transaction fails to meet the criteria for the cheapest [[Interchange|interchange]] rate and gets bumped to a pricier one, often for want of the [[Level 2 & Level 3 Data|Level 2 and Level 3 data]] that would have qualified it.
x: The payment quietly costing the merchant more because some box wasn't ticked just right.
g: Nothing good for the merchant, but understanding it is how they avoid overpaying.

p: A card-pricing model that lumps hundreds of [[Interchange|interchange]] rates into a few simple buckets, usually to the merchant's disadvantage, much like [[Blended Pricing|blended pricing]] and the opposite of transparent [[Interchange-Plus|interchange-plus]].
x: The 'qualified, mid, non-qualified' shell game that hides what you're really paying behind tidy tiers.
g: Simple to read, but it's the pricing model savvy merchants learn to avoid.

p: A legitimate transaction wrongly rejected by the issuer or fraud rules, the [[False Positive|false positive]] of the [[Authorisation|authorisation]] world.
x: Your perfectly good card getting refused, costing the merchant a sale and you your dignity at the till.
g: Nothing good, but cutting false declines recovers huge amounts of lost revenue.

p: Fraudulent volume as a fraction of total volume, usually quoted in basis points, a core risk-economics KPI.
x: The tiny-but-terrifying percentage of payments that turn out to be fraud, watched obsessively by risk teams.
g: Measuring how much fraud a portfolio carries, to price and manage risk.

p: A [[Stablecoin|stablecoin]] that passes the interest earned on its reserves, often tokenised Treasuries, through to holders.
x: A digital dollar that quietly pays you to hold it, which regulators find very interesting indeed.
g: Earning a return on stable holdings, though the rules around it are still unsettled.

p: The point at which a [[Blockchain|blockchain]] transaction becomes irreversible, whether instantly or after enough confirmations, the crypto form of [[Settlement Finality|settlement finality]].
x: The moment a crypto payment is truly done and can't be unwound, no take-backs.
g: Knowing for certain when an on-chain payment is genuinely settled.

p: A cross-border flow of fiat into [[Stablecoin|stablecoin]] and back into fiat, used to settle international payments in minutes.
x: Turning pounds into a stablecoin to zip across a border, then back into dollars, all before lunch.
g: Moving money across borders fast and cheaply using stablecoins as the middle layer.

p: A tokenised form of money, such as a [[CBDC]], [[Tokenised Deposits|tokenised deposit]] or [[Stablecoin|stablecoin]], used to settle the cash leg of an on-chain trade.
x: The on-chain cash that completes a tokenised deal, so asset and money change hands together.
g: Enabling instant, [[Atomic Settlement|atomic settlement]] of tokenised assets against money.

p: The agreed data standard for transmitting [[Travel Rule]] originator and beneficiary information between crypto firms.
x: The common form crypto businesses fill in so the [[Travel Rule]] data actually fits together.
g: Letting [[VASP|VASPs]] exchange compliance data in one consistent format.

p: The breakdown of the [[MDR|merchant discount rate]] into its parts: [[Interchange]], [[Scheme Fees|scheme fees]] and the processor's markup.
x: Cracking open the single 'merchant fee' to see the three different hands it actually feeds.
g: Showing merchants where each slice of their card fee really goes.

p: The share of failed payments that get automatically re-attempted, and how often those retries succeed, a key revenue-recovery metric that works hand in hand with the [[Payment Cascade]] and with [[Dunning]].
x: The quiet measure of how many bounced payments get a second shot before anyone gives up.
g: Measuring how much failed-payment revenue is clawed back through retries.

p: Automatically routing a [[Decline|declined]] payment through another acquirer or [[PSP]] to recover the sale, rather than giving up at the first refusal, a [[Smart Routing|smart routing]] tactic that, alongside the [[Retry Rate|retry rate]], drives revenue recovery.
x: Giving a bounced payment a second (and third) run at a different door until one opens.
g: Recovering revenue from declines by re-routing across providers.

p: The legal selling entity on a transaction, carrying the commercial and tax liability, distinct from the [[Merchant of Record|merchant of record]] that owns the payment relationship.
x: Whoever's name is legally on the sale, and therefore holds the bag if the taxman comes knocking.
g: Pinning down who is legally responsible for a sale and its taxes.

p: The processor that authorises and manages transactions on the [[Issuer|issuing]] side, running the card programme's day-to-day plumbing.
x: The engine room behind a card programme, turning 'we'd like to issue cards' into cards that actually work.
g: Powering modern card issuing without a bank building it all in-house.

p: The entity that runs a card programme end to end, often on a [[Sponsor Bank|sponsor bank's]] licence and [[BIN Sponsorship|BIN]].
x: The conductor of a card programme, orchestrating the bank, processor and brand so a fintech doesn't have to.
g: Letting brands launch cards without becoming a bank themselves.

p: A software platform that embeds payments into its product, often earning a share of the flow, a major channel for [[Embedded Finance|embedded finance]].
x: The booking or invoicing app that quietly became a payments business the moment it added a Pay button.
g: Distributing payments through the software merchants already use every day.

p: A provider that onboards many merchants under its own master account and assumes liability for them, closely related to a [[Payment Facilitator|payment facilitator]].
x: The big umbrella that lets lots of small merchants shelter under one set of payment credentials.
g: Getting small merchants accepting payments fast, without their own full accounts.

p: Reselling another provider's payment product under your own brand, so customers see you, not the engine underneath.
x: Slapping your logo on someone else's payments tech and quietly hoping nobody asks who built it.
g: Launching a branded payments offering without building the technology.

p: Turnkey infrastructure to launch and run branded card programmes via [[API]], the issuing sibling of [[BaaS]].
x: Cards from a tap of an API, so a startup can ship plastic (or virtual) without years of bank-building.
g: Letting any company offer its own cards quickly through ready-made rails.

p: Revenue-generating extras beyond core processing, like data insights, loyalty, [[FX]] or fraud tools, increasingly where the margins live.
