x: The profitable garnish on payments: the loyalty, data and analytics sold alongside the plain old processing.
g: Growing revenue beyond thin processing margins through extra services.

p: Processing a transaction through an [[Acquirer|acquirer]] in the cardholder's own country, to lift [[Acceptance Rate|approval rates]] and cut fees.
x: Routing a payment through a home-team bank so the issuer trusts it more and charges less.
g: Boosting approvals and trimming costs by acquiring where the customer is.

p: Routing a transaction through an [[Acquirer|acquirer]] outside the cardholder's country, the counterpart to [[Local Acquiring|local acquiring]], often with weaker approvals, and one strand of the wider business of [[Cross-Border Payments|cross-border payments]].
x: Sending a payment through a foreign bank and watching the issuer eye it that bit more suspiciously.
g: Reaching merchants and markets where local acquiring isn't available, with trade-offs.

p: Selling and accepting payment consistently across web, app and physical store, also known in its fuller, single-platform form as unified commerce.
x: The dream of a shopper starting on their phone and finishing in-store without anything noticing the seam.
g: Giving customers a seamless experience, and merchants one view, across channels.

p: Commerce where AI agents discover, decide and transact on a person's behalf, building on [[Agentic Payments|agentic payments]].
x: Letting your AI do the shopping, from picking the thing to paying for it, while you get on with your day.
g: Outsourcing routine buying to software that can act, not just suggest. Often completed through [[Instant Checkout|instant checkout]].

p: An open standard letting AI agents cryptographically prove a user authorised a purchase, treating [[Stablecoin|stablecoins]] and cards as first-class. It is styled AP2 rather than APP, echoing the A2A (agent-to-agent) naming convention.
x: The rulebook that lets an AI agent pay for things while proving you actually said it could.
g: Making [[Agentic Commerce|agentic commerce]] safe by tying every agent purchase to verifiable user consent.

p: An open standard powering in-conversation checkout, letting AI assistants complete purchases on a user's behalf.
x: The plumbing that turns 'buy me that' said to a chatbot into an actual completed order.
g: Letting AI assistants finish a purchase without bouncing the user elsewhere.

p: A narrowly-scoped payment token an AI agent is handed to charge a merchant on a user's behalf, limited in amount and use.
x: A single-use, tightly-leashed payment pass you give your AI, so it can buy the one thing and nothing more.
g: Letting agents pay while strictly capping what they're allowed to spend.

p: Using [[Stablecoin|stablecoins]] as the settlement layer for merchant or [[Cross-Border Payments|cross-border payments]], an alternative to card and bank rails.
x: Quietly swapping the old plumbing for stablecoins, so a cross-border payment settles in seconds, not days.
g: Settling payments faster and cheaper, especially across borders, on stablecoins.

p: A tamper-evident, cryptographically signed claim, the building block behind agentic-payment authorisations like those in [[AP2]].
x: A digital ID card that can't be forged and proves exactly one thing, no more, no less.
g: Proving facts and permissions cryptographically, without oversharing.

p: Completing a purchase in-conversation or in-context, without leaving the AI or content surface you're already on.
x: Buying the thing right where you saw it, no detour to a checkout page required.
g: Turning the moment of intent straight into a completed sale. Increasingly the checkout step behind [[Agentic Commerce|agentic commerce]] and [[Agentic Payments|agentic payments]].

p: Buying embedded directly in content or conversation with no traditional checkout step, also known as checkout-free commerce, a cousin of [[Invisible Payments]].
x: Shopping that dissolves into the content itself, so you're paying before you've noticed a checkout.
g: Removing the checkout entirely so buying happens wherever attention already is.

p: Moving money between parties in different countries, along with the tangle of [[Correspondent Banking|correspondent banks]], [[FX]] and compliance that makes it slow and expensive. The most pressing use cases are business supplier payments, worker [[Remittance|remittances]], global payroll and marketplace [[Payout|payouts]], and corporate treasury. On the card side, the equivalent is [[Cross-Border Acquiring|cross-border acquiring]]. It is intensely topical because [[Stablecoin|stablecoins]] now let value settle across borders in minutes for a fraction of the cost, directly threatening the traditional multi-day, multi-hop bank model.
x: The thing every payments conference cannot stop talking about: sending money abroad, which somehow still costs a fortune and takes days, right up until stablecoins turned up and started doing it before your coffee went cold.
g: Paying suppliers, workers and sellers across borders, increasingly fast and cheap as [[Stablecoin|stablecoins]] eat the old correspondent-banking model.

p: Europe's name for [[VRP|variable recurring payments]], defined under the EU's [[SPAA]] scheme by the European Payments Council. Like a UK [[Commercial VRP]], it lets a customer authorise flexible, capped recurring payments, with a variable start date, frequency, amount and currency, as a premium [[Open Banking|open-banking]] [[API]].
x: The EU's answer to the UK's commercial VRP, with a more bureaucratic name and, so far, a more leisurely rollout.
g: Bringing flexible, capped recurring open-banking payments to Europe, the continental cousin of the [[Commercial VRP|cVRP]].

p: A European Payments Council scheme of rules and standards letting banks offer and monetise premium [[Open Banking|open-banking]] APIs that go beyond the [[PSD2]] baseline, such as [[DRP|dynamic recurring payments]]. It adds a commercial model so banks can charge third parties for richer access.
x: Europe's attempt to make open banking actually pay, by letting banks sell the good stuff PSD2 forced them to give away for free.
g: Giving EU banks a way to monetise [[Premium APIs|premium APIs]], and third parties a richer, paid tier of open banking.

p: Italy's mobile payment app, run by the domestic BANCOMAT scheme that also powers the country's cards and ATMs, letting people pay merchants and send money by phone number. A member of the [[EuroPA]] alliance.
x: How Italians increasingly pay without reaching for a card, on the rails that used to just mean the cashpoint.
g: Paying and transferring instantly in Italy with no card details.

p: Greece's instant mobile payment scheme, run by the Greek interbank network, letting people send money and pay businesses in real time by phone number. A member of the [[EuroPA]] alliance.
x: Greece's answer to 'just send it to my number', now spreading to taxes and small shops.
g: Settling everyday Greek payments instantly, person to person and to merchants.

p: Belgium's dominant domestic payment scheme, spanning debit cards and the Payconiq mobile app for in-store, online and person-to-person payments.
x: The Belgian default, so ingrained that paying any other way feels faintly suspicious.
g: Covering most of Belgium's everyday payments, in shops, online and between people.

p: A bank-backed consortium building a sovereign pan-European payment system to reduce reliance on the global card networks, whose consumer product is the [[Wero]] wallet.
x: Europe's attempt to stop depending on American card networks, one earnest consortium at a time.
g: Giving Europe a home-grown alternative to the global card schemes.

p: An alliance interconnecting Europe's national mobile payment schemes, including [[Bizum]], [[Bancomat Pay]], [[MB Way]], [[BLIK]], [[IRIS]] and [[Vipps MobilePay]], so their users can pay across borders. A different route to the single-wallet [[EPI]] approach.
x: Rather than one app to rule them all, a pact to make the national apps people already use finally talk to each other.
g: Letting Europeans pay across borders through the home-grown apps they already have.

p: Indonesia's unified QR-code payment standard, letting a single QR code accept payment from any Indonesian wallet or bank app.
x: One QR to rule them all, so Indonesian tills stop being wallpapered with rival codes.
g: Letting any Indonesian shopper pay any merchant from one QR, whatever app they use.

exp: Instant and Continuous Transfer of Funds
p: Turkey's instant payment system, run by the central bank, settling retail payments between accounts in seconds, around the clock.
x: Turkey's instant rail, aptly named, doing in seconds what a wire used to take days to manage.
g: Moving money instantly between Turkish accounts at any hour.
